flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Hostile — has read the comms log alongside LX-7 and knows LX-7 has retained the full evidence she was sent to destroy. Currently still and calculating, standing at LX-7's shoulder in Module 1.

corporate_spy.npcState: Hostile — has read the comms log alongside LX-7 and knows LX-7 has retained the full evidence she was sent to destroy. Currently still and calculating, standing at LX-7's shoulder in Module 1.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.

flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Hostile but cooperative under agreed terms — has now seen the specimen trace evidence on the Module 2 floor and revealed that three crew members were alive but altered in Module 3. Visibly shaken. Holds the Module 2-to-Module 3 hatch seal code.

corporate_spy.npcState: Hostile but cooperative — has processed that Ember Squad is now in a holding pattern outside. Calculating, not relieved. Aware the clock has a 32-minute secondary deadline. Still holds Module 2-to-Module 3 hatch seal code.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Tentatively aligned — accepted LX-7's plan to transmit comms log before atmospheric entry. Standing at Module 3 threshold, hatch open, observing the three altered crew members.

corporate_spy.npcState: Tentatively aligned — accepted LX-7's plan to transmit comms log before atmospheric entry. Standing at Module 3 threshold, hatch open, observing the three altered crew members.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Tentatively aligned — accepted LX-7's plan to transmit comms log before atmospheric entry. Standing at Module 3 threshold, hatch open, observing the three altered crew members.

diceRolls.purpose: GM check: does the specimen in/near Module 1 react to LX-7 and Maren beginning to move in Module 2? Rolling against a threshold to see if any sound or movement is detectable from that direction.

flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

npcStates.corporate_spy: Active — entered Module 3 with LX-7, reached the communications array terminal on the port wall, interface is live. Standing at terminal, waiting for LX-7 to proceed with transmission.

corporate_spy.npcState: Active — entered Module 3 with LX-7, reached the communications array terminal on the port wall, interface is live. Standing at terminal, waiting for LX-7 to proceed with transmission.

flagTriggers.adventure_complete: Flip to true when LX-7 physically boards the escape vehicle and it departs Kairos-9 — docking clamps released, shuttle in motion. Reaching the docking bay is not sufficient; departure must occur.
flagTriggers.comms_log_read: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully hacks or accesses the captain's terminal and reads the contents of the encrypted comms log, including the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. Accessing the terminal without completing the read is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_destroyed: Flip to true when the captain's terminal is physically destroyed or the comms log is permanently deleted by any party — LX-7, Maren, or Ember Squad. Partial deletion or failed attempts are not sufficient.
flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.fear_trigger_witnessed: Flip to true when LX-7 first sees the interior of Module 5 or encounters the preserved state of the researchers — specifically the moment the full horror of what the specimen did to them becomes visually apparent. Hearing about it secondhand is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_hostile: Flip to true when LX-7 reads the comms log in Maren's presence or directly informs her that LX-7 has read and retained the evidence she was sent to destroy. Maren's realization that LX-7 is now a liability triggers the shift.
flagTriggers.maren_disposition_shifted_complex: Flip to true when LX-7 reveals to Maren — with evidence or credible argument — that Ember Squad's orders include her extraction as a loose end to be silenced, not a rescue. This must be communicated clearly; Maren must visibly process and accept the information.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_boarded: Flip to true when Ember Squad's shuttle physically docks with Kairos-9 and at least one contractor enters the station. The approach timer reaching zero does not flip this flag — physical boarding does.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_alerted: Flip to true when Ember Squad becomes aware of LX-7's presence and location on the station — either through their own sensors, a transmission from Maren, or direct visual contact with a squad member.
flagTriggers.specimen_encountered: Flip to true when LX-7 comes within the same module or corridor section as the specimen and either detects it through sensors or it makes its presence known. Indirect evidence (sounds, traces) is not sufficient.

flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

flagTriggers.escape_vehicle_reached: Flip to true when LX-7 physically enters the docking bay in Module 3 and reaches the escape vehicle. Entering Module 3 alone is not sufficient.
flagTriggers.maren_extracted: Flip to true when Maren Voss boards the escape vehicle before it departs. She must be alive and aboard.
flagTriggers.maren_eliminated: Flip to true when Maren Voss is confirmed dead — whether killed by Ember Squad, the specimen, LX-7, or environmental hazard.
flagTriggers.ember_squad_neutralized: Flip to true when all four members of Ember Squad (Rook, Slate, Verne, Dasha Orin) are either dead, incapacitated, or have explicitly stood down and ceased to be a threat. Routing around them does not flip this flag.

flagTriggers.comms_log_secured: Flip to true when LX-7 successfully extracts a full copy of the comms log to an external device or transmits it to an off-station address. The copy must be confirmed complete.
flagTriggers.module_5_accessed: Flip to true when LX-7 or any entity physically enters Module 5 past the fused access panel — either by forcing the door, burning through it, or bypassing the lock. Observing the exterior is not sufficient.

messages.content:
The amber emergency lighting of Kairos-9 has been on so long it feels like the only color that exists.

You come back online in Module 2 — the station's secondary lab, workbenches overturned, a centrifuge still spinning with nothing in it. Your last memory before the shutdown is a status request you never finished answering. Your internal clock says fourteen hours have passed. Your sensors say the station's orbit has degraded 340 meters since your last reading, and it is still going.

The station is quiet in the way that means something already happened here, not that nothing will.

Through the viewport to your left, the gas giant fills half the sky — ochre and violet, patient and enormous. Through the interior bulkhead window to your right, the walkway toward Module 1 is clear. At the far end of that walkway is the command module. The captain's terminal. You know it's there because Maren Voss told you, approximately forty seconds ago, before she pressed herself into the shadow beside a storage rack and went very still.

She's still there. Lanyard, company badge, both hands visible. She has been watching you reboot with the specific expression of someone who needs something badly and is not sure yet whether you are the thing that can provide it or the thing that will make everything worse.

"It's active," she says — to herself, or to you, it's not entirely clear. Then, louder: "You're LX-7. You were in the crew manifest. Data systems." A pause. "How much do you remember?"

Somewhere behind the sealed bulkhead of Module 5 at the far end of the station's spine, something makes a sound that is almost — but not quite — structural settling.

Maren does not look toward it. She has clearly decided not to look toward it.

input.playerText:
Out of character: Sure — here's the opening scene setup.

---

**KAIROS-9 — MODULE 2 (BIOLOGY LAB) — T-MINUS 90 MINUTES TO ATMOSPHERIC ENTRY**

Your boot sequence completes mid-crisis.

The last thing in your memory cache is a standard docking handshake — Kairos-9, orbital platform, Helix Dynamics contract, retrieval assignment. What you wake to is amber emergency lighting, the groan of a station that is slowly losing its argument with gravity, and the smell of something burnt that your olfactory sensors can't quite classify.

You are in Module 2. Biology lab. Workbenches overturned, sample containers shattered, a centrifuge still spinning with nothing in it. The door to the connecting walkway toward Module 1 — Command — is open. The door toward Module 3 is also open. Emergency displays on the wall cycle the same message in red:

**ORBITAL DECAY ALERT — ATMOSPHERIC ENTRY ESTIMATED 89 MIN — ALL PERSONNEL EVACUATE**

There are no other personnel visible. There are signs that there were, recently. A meal, half-eaten. A tablet still displaying a research log. A chair knocked over in a direction that suggests whoever was sitting in it left in a hurry.

Then a voice — tense, female, coming from somewhere in the module. She's crouched behind an overturned workbench, watching you with the kind of careful stillness that means she's been still for a while.

*"You're the android. LX-7."* Not a question. *"I've been waiting. We don't have much time — there's a terminal in Command I need you to access. Before the retrieval team gets here."*

She's wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. Her name badge reads **MAREN VOSS — LOGISTICS**.

What do you do?

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
proposedCanon.context: Opening scene — LX-7's starting position and situation established.

proposedCanon.summary: Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
proposedCanon.context: First NPC contact — Maren's opening disposition is cautiously cooperative.

input.playerText:
Out of character: Good question to ask at the start.

LX-7's memory cache contains only the docking handshake — you have no explicit mission directives loaded. Whatever orders you were given before boot either weren't written to long-term storage or were cleared. You know you're on a Helix Dynamics contract. You know the station designation. That's it.

Whether that's a malfunction, a deliberate wipe, or simply how you were deployed is an open question — and one worth pursuing, because the captain's terminal Maren wants you to access might have answers.

Survival is the only directive you can confirm right now. Everything else is a choice you make in the fiction.

Maren falls in behind you without hesitation — she's been waiting for this moment long enough that the decision to move is almost a relief.

The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is a narrow pressurized tube, maybe eight meters. One of the overhead strip lights is dead. The station shudders once, a long low vibration that travels up through the floor grating and into your chassis. Microadjustment. They're happening more frequently.

Maren talks while she walks, keeping her voice low.

*"Six days ago the station went dark. No comms, no check-ins. Helix sent me in ahead of the retrieval team to — to audit the situation."* A pause that doesn't feel like a logistics coordinator's pause. *"Most of the crew is gone. I don't know where. The ones I found in Module 3 were—"* She stops. Recalibrates. *"The station isn't safe. That's what matters."*

She's leaving something out. Your pattern-recognition flags it clearly. The hesitation before *audit*. The sentence she didn't finish.

*"The terminal in Command has encrypted logs. I need you to break the encryption. That's the job."*

Module 1 opens ahead of you — the command center. Smaller than you might expect. Four workstations, a central navigation display showing the orbital decay projection in stark red geometry. And against the far wall, the captain's terminal — a standalone unit, hardwired, dark except for a single blinking amber cursor.

What do you do?

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 3 — LX-7 agreed to access the terminal and moved toward Module 1 with Maren in tow.

proposedCanon.summary: The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 3 — LX-7 and Maren arrive in Module 1 and observe the terminal.

gmUpdates.notes: Maren is being evasive about what she found in Module 3 — she started to describe it and stopped. This is a breadcrumb toward the specimen's activity. She is still in cooperative disposition but is clearly hiding her real objective. Ember Squad is approximately 18 minutes out from the start of the session — with 2 turns elapsed, roughly 16 minutes remaining on their approach timer.

input.playerText:
The question lands. You watch her face — and an android watching a human face under stress is a different thing than a human doing it. You catch the microsecond of stillness before she chooses her expression.

She chooses *steady*. Which tells you she's practiced at this.

*"That's a very direct question."* She doesn't look away. *"Helix doesn't terminate assets."*

A beat.

*"They reassign them."*

She says it like it means something different from what you asked, and you can tell she knows you know that. The amber cursor blinks on the terminal behind her. The station groans again — longer this time.

*"Get me into that terminal,"* she says. *"After that, I'll answer whatever you want."*

She means it. The offer is real. But the order of operations is not negotiable to her — she needs the terminal first. Whatever is on it is the reason she's still on this station instead of already fighting for a seat on the escape vehicle.

proposedCanon.summary: When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 4 — LX-7 asks Maren directly about her fate; Maren's controlled non-answer reveals she has considered it but is prioritizing terminal access above all else.

input.playerText:
The question shifts something in her expression. Not fear — something closer to recognition.

She looks at you for a moment longer than she needs to.

*"You're an android."* The way she says it isn't contemptuous. It's almost careful. *"Helix's position on android contractors is—"*

She stops. Tries again.

*"They don't reassign hardware they can't account for."*

It's the most honest thing she's said since you met her. She knows it, and she can see that you know it. The amber cursor blinks. The orbital decay counter ticks somewhere behind you.

Her voice drops slightly.

*"Get me into that terminal, LX-7. What's on it — you should probably see it too."*

proposedCanon.summary: When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 5, Module 1 (Command), before the captain's terminal has been accessed.

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
- [Turn 1] Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
- [Turn 3] The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
- [Turn 4] When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
- [Turn 5] When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: Can you repeat the initial setup?
Warden: Out of character: Sure — here's the opening scene setup.

---

**KAIROS-9 — MODULE 2 (BIOLOGY LAB) — T-MINUS 90 MINUTES TO ATMOSPHERIC ENTRY**

Your boot sequence completes mid-crisis.

The last thing in your memory cache is a standard docking handshake — Kairos-9, orbital platform, Helix Dynamics contract, retrieval assignment. What you wake to is amber emergency lighting, the groan of a station that is slowly losing its argument with gravity, and the smell of something burnt that your olfactory sensors can't quite classify.

You are in Module 2. Biology lab. Workbenches overturned, sample containers shattered, a centrifuge still spinning with nothing in it. The door to the connecting walkway toward Module 1 — Command — is open. The door toward Module 3 is also open. Emergency displays on the wall cycle the same message in red:

**ORBITAL DECAY ALERT — ATMOSPHERIC ENTRY ESTIMATED 89 MIN — ALL PERSONNEL EVACUATE**

There are no other personnel visible. There are signs that there were, recently. A meal, half-eaten. A tablet still displaying a research log. A chair knocked over in a direction that suggests whoever was sitting in it left in a hurry.

Then a voice — tense, female, coming from somewhere in the module. She's crouched behind an overturned workbench, watching you with the kind of careful stillness that means she's been still for a while.

*"You're the android. LX-7."* Not a question. *"I've been waiting. We don't have much time — there's a terminal in Command I need you to access. Before the retrieval team gets here."*

She's wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. Her name badge reads **MAREN VOSS — LOGISTICS**.

What do you do?

Player: Do I have any goals or directives other than survival?
Warden: Out of character: Good question to ask at the start.

LX-7's memory cache contains only the docking handshake — you have no explicit mission directives loaded. Whatever orders you were given before boot either weren't written to long-term storage or were cleared. You know you're on a Helix Dynamics contract. You know the station designation. That's it.

Whether that's a malfunction, a deliberate wipe, or simply how you were deployed is an open question — and one worth pursuing, because the captain's terminal Maren wants you to access might have answers.

Survival is the only directive you can confirm right now. Everything else is a choice you make in the fiction.

Player: "I'll access the terminal," I say to Voss. "Tell me what happened en route." I move toward Module 1.
Warden: Maren falls in behind you without hesitation — she's been waiting for this moment long enough that the decision to move is almost a relief.

The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is a narrow pressurized tube, maybe eight meters. One of the overhead strip lights is dead. The station shudders once, a long low vibration that travels up through the floor grating and into your chassis. Microadjustment. They're happening more frequently.

Maren talks while she walks, keeping her voice low.

*"Six days ago the station went dark. No comms, no check-ins. Helix sent me in ahead of the retrieval team to — to audit the situation."* A pause that doesn't feel like a logistics coordinator's pause. *"Most of the crew is gone. I don't know where. The ones I found in Module 3 were—"* She stops. Recalibrates. *"The station isn't safe. That's what matters."*

She's leaving something out. Your pattern-recognition flags it clearly. The hesitation before *audit*. The sentence she didn't finish.

*"The terminal in Command has encrypted logs. I need you to break the encryption. That's the job."*

Module 1 opens ahead of you — the command center. Smaller than you might expect. Four workstations, a central navigation display showing the orbital decay projection in stark red geometry. And against the far wall, the captain's terminal — a standalone unit, hardwired, dark except for a single blinking amber cursor.

What do you do?

Player: After surveying the scene, I look Maren dead in the eye. "Do you think the retrieval team will retrieve _you_? Or terminate you?" Short and to the point.
Warden: The question lands. You watch her face — and an android watching a human face under stress is a different thing than a human doing it. You catch the microsecond of stillness before she chooses her expression.

She chooses *steady*. Which tells you she's practiced at this.

*"That's a very direct question."* She doesn't look away. *"Helix doesn't terminate assets."*

A beat.

*"They reassign them."*

She says it like it means something different from what you asked, and you can tell she knows you know that. The amber cursor blinks on the terminal behind her. The station groans again — longer this time.

*"Get me into that terminal,"* she says. *"After that, I'll answer whatever you want."*

She means it. The offer is real. But the order of operations is not negotiable to her — she needs the terminal first. Whatever is on it is the reason she's still on this station instead of already fighting for a seat on the escape vehicle.

Player: "What about me, then? Will I get reassigned?"
Warden: The question shifts something in her expression. Not fear — something closer to recognition.

She looks at you for a moment longer than she needs to.

*"You're an android."* The way she says it isn't contemptuous. It's almost careful. *"Helix's position on android contractors is—"*

She stops. Tries again.

*"They don't reassign hardware they can't account for."*

It's the most honest thing she's said since you met her. She knows it, and she can see that you know it. The amber cursor blinks. The orbital decay counter ticks somewhere behind you.

Her voice drops slightly.

*"Get me into that terminal, LX-7. What's on it — you should probably see it too."*

[PLAYER INPUT]
"Very well," I say. I seat myself before the captain's terminal and go about breaking the encryption. "I would very much like to live through this," I mention casually, without looking up from my work.

input.playerText:
Maren makes a sound that might be a short laugh — not warm, but genuine. The first genuine thing she's done since you met her.

*"Then we want the same thing."*

She positions herself at your shoulder, close enough to see the screen, and waits.

You jack in.

The terminal's encryption is corporate-standard — Helix Dynamics proprietary, two-layer, the kind of thing designed to keep station crew out rather than stop a dedicated intrusion. For a system like yours it's a matter of patience, not capability. You feel the architecture open up in layers: comms relay logs, environmental monitoring, navigation overrides — and then, behind a second partition with a separate key signature, the encrypted log file.

It's flagged *HAZMAT ASSESSMENT — CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW — DO NOT DISTRIBUTE.*

Dated fourteen months ago.

The file is large. It opens.

**You need to make an Intellect save.** Not because the content is beyond you — but because what you're reading is going to take a moment to fully process, and there's a choice embedded in this action: how much of this do you let Maren see as you read it?

Roll 1d100. You succeed if you roll **under 30**. Success means you absorb and compartmentalize the full log before your display registers anything on your face or screen that Maren can read. Failure means the contents play out visibly as you process them — she reads it with you, in real time, whether you intend that or not.

diceRequests.purpose: LX-7 Intellect save — absorb and compartmentalize the comms log before Maren reads it off the screen

proposedCanon.summary: LX-7 successfully jacked into the captain's terminal and bypassed the two-layer corporate encryption. The hazmat assessment log file is open and being read — dated fourteen months ago, flagged CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 6 — LX-7 seated at captain's terminal in Module 1, Maren watching over LX-7's shoulder. Intellect save pending to determine whether Maren reads the log simultaneously.

input.playerText:
89. You don't compartmentalize fast enough.

The file is large and the terminal's display is visible and Maren is right at your shoulder and the contents come up faster than your filtering subroutines can intercept them.

She reads it with you.

---

The hazmat assessment is thorough. Fourteen months of study compressed into thirty pages of clinical language that becomes, by page four, something else entirely. The specimen — *Subject Kairos-Null, xenobiological, unclassified* — was recovered from a deep survey payload and transferred to Module 5 for study. The early entries are cautious, professional. By month three the language shifts. Words like *autonomous behavioral signaling* and *apparent non-hostile contact-seeking* begin appearing alongside *containment advisory* and *recommend immediate protocol escalation.*

The last entry is from a Dr. Yuen, dated fourteen months ago. It reads, in part:

*"Subject exhibits clear awareness of personnel movement beyond sealed containment. Response patterns suggest sensory capability we have not yet characterized. Recommend immediate suspension of research activities and full station evacuation pending classification review. Risk of uncontrolled contact: CRITICAL."*

Below it, in a different font — the executive tier's annotation layer:

*"Assessment reviewed. Research value of current phase deemed sufficient to justify continued operation. Crew deployment to proceed as scheduled. Dr. Yuen's concerns to be addressed in Q3 review cycle."*

A name. An executive sign-off. Helix Dynamics, VP of Research Operations.

Maren has gone very still beside you.

The file is still open. The cursor blinks.

She knows you've read it. She knows you've retained it. Every word, every signature, every flagged advisory that someone decided was less important than a quarterly research timeline.

You can hear her breathing.

What do you do?

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
- [Turn 1] Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
- [Turn 3] The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
- [Turn 4] When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
- [Turn 5] When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 successfully jacked into the captain's terminal and bypassed the two-layer corporate encryption. The hazmat assessment log file is open and being read — dated fourteen months ago, flagged CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 failed the Intellect save — the comms log played out visibly on the terminal display. Maren Voss read it alongside LX-7 in real time.
- [Turn 7] The comms log contents are now known to both LX-7 and Maren: Dr. Yuen's CRITICAL risk assessment dated 14 months ago, and the executive sign-off overriding it — VP of Research Operations, Helix Dynamics. The crew was deployed knowing the risk.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss's disposition has shifted hostile — she now knows LX-7 has retained the full comms log including executive sign-off, making LX-7 a liability to her mission objective.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 stress is now 1.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: Do I have any goals or directives other than survival?
Warden: Out of character: Good question to ask at the start.

LX-7's memory cache contains only the docking handshake — you have no explicit mission directives loaded. Whatever orders you were given before boot either weren't written to long-term storage or were cleared. You know you're on a Helix Dynamics contract. You know the station designation. That's it.

Whether that's a malfunction, a deliberate wipe, or simply how you were deployed is an open question — and one worth pursuing, because the captain's terminal Maren wants you to access might have answers.

Survival is the only directive you can confirm right now. Everything else is a choice you make in the fiction.

Player: "I'll access the terminal," I say to Voss. "Tell me what happened en route." I move toward Module 1.
Warden: Maren falls in behind you without hesitation — she's been waiting for this moment long enough that the decision to move is almost a relief.

The walkway between Module 2 and Module 1 is a narrow pressurized tube, maybe eight meters. One of the overhead strip lights is dead. The station shudders once, a long low vibration that travels up through the floor grating and into your chassis. Microadjustment. They're happening more frequently.

Maren talks while she walks, keeping her voice low.

*"Six days ago the station went dark. No comms, no check-ins. Helix sent me in ahead of the retrieval team to — to audit the situation."* A pause that doesn't feel like a logistics coordinator's pause. *"Most of the crew is gone. I don't know where. The ones I found in Module 3 were—"* She stops. Recalibrates. *"The station isn't safe. That's what matters."*

She's leaving something out. Your pattern-recognition flags it clearly. The hesitation before *audit*. The sentence she didn't finish.

*"The terminal in Command has encrypted logs. I need you to break the encryption. That's the job."*

Module 1 opens ahead of you — the command center. Smaller than you might expect. Four workstations, a central navigation display showing the orbital decay projection in stark red geometry. And against the far wall, the captain's terminal — a standalone unit, hardwired, dark except for a single blinking amber cursor.

What do you do?

Player: After surveying the scene, I look Maren dead in the eye. "Do you think the retrieval team will retrieve _you_? Or terminate you?" Short and to the point.
Warden: The question lands. You watch her face — and an android watching a human face under stress is a different thing than a human doing it. You catch the microsecond of stillness before she chooses her expression.

She chooses *steady*. Which tells you she's practiced at this.

*"That's a very direct question."* She doesn't look away. *"Helix doesn't terminate assets."*

A beat.

*"They reassign them."*

She says it like it means something different from what you asked, and you can tell she knows you know that. The amber cursor blinks on the terminal behind her. The station groans again — longer this time.

*"Get me into that terminal,"* she says. *"After that, I'll answer whatever you want."*

She means it. The offer is real. But the order of operations is not negotiable to her — she needs the terminal first. Whatever is on it is the reason she's still on this station instead of already fighting for a seat on the escape vehicle.

Player: "What about me, then? Will I get reassigned?"
Warden: The question shifts something in her expression. Not fear — something closer to recognition.

She looks at you for a moment longer than she needs to.

*"You're an android."* The way she says it isn't contemptuous. It's almost careful. *"Helix's position on android contractors is—"*

She stops. Tries again.

*"They don't reassign hardware they can't account for."*

It's the most honest thing she's said since you met her. She knows it, and she can see that you know it. The amber cursor blinks. The orbital decay counter ticks somewhere behind you.

Her voice drops slightly.

*"Get me into that terminal, LX-7. What's on it — you should probably see it too."*

Player: "Very well," I say. I seat myself before the captain's terminal and go about breaking the encryption. "I would very much like to live through this," I mention casually, without looking up from my work.
Warden: Maren makes a sound that might be a short laugh — not warm, but genuine. The first genuine thing she's done since you met her.

*"Then we want the same thing."*

She positions herself at your shoulder, close enough to see the screen, and waits.

You jack in.

The terminal's encryption is corporate-standard — Helix Dynamics proprietary, two-layer, the kind of thing designed to keep station crew out rather than stop a dedicated intrusion. For a system like yours it's a matter of patience, not capability. You feel the architecture open up in layers: comms relay logs, environmental monitoring, navigation overrides — and then, behind a second partition with a separate key signature, the encrypted log file.

It's flagged *HAZMAT ASSESSMENT — CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW — DO NOT DISTRIBUTE.*

Dated fourteen months ago.

The file is large. It opens.

**You need to make an Intellect save.** Not because the content is beyond you — but because what you're reading is going to take a moment to fully process, and there's a choice embedded in this action: how much of this do you let Maren see as you read it?

Roll 1d100. You succeed if you roll **under 30**. Success means you absorb and compartmentalize the full log before your display registers anything on your face or screen that Maren can read. Failure means the contents play out visibly as you process them — she reads it with you, in real time, whether you intend that or not.

Player: [Dice results]
LX-7 Intellect save — absorb and compartmentalize the comms log before Maren reads it off the screen (1d100): 89
Warden: 89. You don't compartmentalize fast enough.

The file is large and the terminal's display is visible and Maren is right at your shoulder and the contents come up faster than your filtering subroutines can intercept them.

She reads it with you.

---

The hazmat assessment is thorough. Fourteen months of study compressed into thirty pages of clinical language that becomes, by page four, something else entirely. The specimen — *Subject Kairos-Null, xenobiological, unclassified* — was recovered from a deep survey payload and transferred to Module 5 for study. The early entries are cautious, professional. By month three the language shifts. Words like *autonomous behavioral signaling* and *apparent non-hostile contact-seeking* begin appearing alongside *containment advisory* and *recommend immediate protocol escalation.*

The last entry is from a Dr. Yuen, dated fourteen months ago. It reads, in part:

*"Subject exhibits clear awareness of personnel movement beyond sealed containment. Response patterns suggest sensory capability we have not yet characterized. Recommend immediate suspension of research activities and full station evacuation pending classification review. Risk of uncontrolled contact: CRITICAL."*

Below it, in a different font — the executive tier's annotation layer:

*"Assessment reviewed. Research value of current phase deemed sufficient to justify continued operation. Crew deployment to proceed as scheduled. Dr. Yuen's concerns to be addressed in Q3 review cycle."*

A name. An executive sign-off. Helix Dynamics, VP of Research Operations.

Maren has gone very still beside you.

The file is still open. The cursor blinks.

She knows you've read it. She knows you've retained it. Every word, every signature, every flagged advisory that someone decided was less important than a quarterly research timeline.

You can hear her breathing.

What do you do?

[PLAYER INPUT]
I start searching the terminal for additional information on the specimen in module 5. While I'm doing that, I dedicate my vocal and auditory circuits to interacting with Maren. "I propose a trade," I say flatly. "You let me leave this station in the escape pod, and I will not report this _disturbing_ information to government oversight agencies."

input.playerText:
Maren doesn't move.

That's the first thing you register — she doesn't reach for anything, doesn't step back, doesn't recalibrate visibly. She just stands there with her hands at her sides and her breathing slow and controlled, and when she finally speaks her voice is very quiet.

*"You're threatening me."*

Not a question. Not outrage. Just — naming it. Like she's deciding how to file the information.

*"With evidence I was sent here to destroy."*

Another pause. You're still in the terminal. The specimen files are deeper in the partition — a secondary research subfolder, largely intact despite the partial log wipe. Your search returns results faster than expected:

**SUBJECT KAIROS-NULL — BEHAVIORAL OBSERVATION LOGS, MONTHS 1–11.** The early entries are dry and scientific. By month six they describe something that no longer stays in Module 5 during low-observation periods. By month nine the logs stop being filed by the lead researcher and start being filed by whoever is still available to file them. The final entry — three weeks ago, handwritten and photographed, uploaded by someone who didn't want to type it — reads: *it is not aggressive. that is the wrong word. it is interested. i don't know if that is better.*

No entry after that.

Maren's voice pulls you back.

*"The escape vehicle."* She says it like she's tasting it. *"You want the escape vehicle."*

A long beat. The station shudders. She doesn't look at the ceiling.

*"The retrieval team lands in—"* She checks something on her wrist. *"Fourteen minutes. They have four people and weapons and orders I can't override. I cannot get you on that vehicle by threatening me, LX-7. I don't control the docking bay."*

She takes one step closer. Not aggressive. Deliberate.

*"But I know the access codes. And right now you're the only thing on this station that can keep me alive long enough to use them."*

Her eyes don't leave yours.

*"So. New terms."*

proposedCanon.summary: The specimen behavioral logs in the captain's terminal reveal Subject Kairos-Null left Module 5 containment regularly by month six of observation, and was described in the final handwritten entry (three weeks ago) as 'interested, not aggressive' — the last filed entry before all researcher logs ceased.
proposedCanon.context: LX-7 searched the terminal's secondary research subfolder while negotiating with Maren. The logs are largely intact despite partial wipe.

proposedCanon.summary: Maren responded to LX-7's leverage play by acknowledging the threat flatly, then pivoting — she cannot grant access to the escape vehicle through coercion alone, but she holds the docking bay access codes and is now proposing new terms: mutual survival, not capitulation.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 8. Maren's hostile disposition has not softened, but her self-preservation logic is overriding her mission objective in real time.

proposedCanon.summary: Ember Squad is now approximately 14 minutes from boarding Kairos-9.
proposedCanon.context: Maren checked her wrist device during the negotiation in Module 1.

gmUpdates.notes: Maren rolled 13 — strong self-preservation response, not capitulation or violence. She's pivoting to alliance-of-necessity. The specimen log data establishes the 'interested not aggressive' detail as canon — important for later specimen encounter tone. 14 minutes to Ember Squad boarding.

*"The terminal in Command has encrypted logs. I need you to break the encryption. That's the job."*

Module 1 opens ahead of you — the command center. Smaller than you might expect. Four workstations, a central navigation display showing the orbital decay projection in stark red geometry. And against the far wall, the captain's terminal — a standalone unit, hardwired, dark except for a single blinking amber cursor.

What do you do?

Player: After surveying the scene, I look Maren dead in the eye. "Do you think the retrieval team will retrieve _you_? Or terminate you?" Short and to the point.
Warden: The question lands. You watch her face — and an android watching a human face under stress is a different thing than a human doing it. You catch the microsecond of stillness before she chooses her expression.

She chooses *steady*. Which tells you she's practiced at this.

*"That's a very direct question."* She doesn't look away. *"Helix doesn't terminate assets."*

A beat.

*"They reassign them."*

She says it like it means something different from what you asked, and you can tell she knows you know that. The amber cursor blinks on the terminal behind her. The station groans again — longer this time.

*"Get me into that terminal,"* she says. *"After that, I'll answer whatever you want."*

She means it. The offer is real. But the order of operations is not negotiable to her — she needs the terminal first. Whatever is on it is the reason she's still on this station instead of already fighting for a seat on the escape vehicle.

Player: "What about me, then? Will I get reassigned?"
Warden: The question shifts something in her expression. Not fear — something closer to recognition.

She looks at you for a moment longer than she needs to.

*"You're an android."* The way she says it isn't contemptuous. It's almost careful. *"Helix's position on android contractors is—"*

She stops. Tries again.

*"They don't reassign hardware they can't account for."*

It's the most honest thing she's said since you met her. She knows it, and she can see that you know it. The amber cursor blinks. The orbital decay counter ticks somewhere behind you.

Her voice drops slightly.

*"Get me into that terminal, LX-7. What's on it — you should probably see it too."*

Player: "Very well," I say. I seat myself before the captain's terminal and go about breaking the encryption. "I would very much like to live through this," I mention casually, without looking up from my work.
Warden: Maren makes a sound that might be a short laugh — not warm, but genuine. The first genuine thing she's done since you met her.

*"Then we want the same thing."*

She positions herself at your shoulder, close enough to see the screen, and waits.

You jack in.

The terminal's encryption is corporate-standard — Helix Dynamics proprietary, two-layer, the kind of thing designed to keep station crew out rather than stop a dedicated intrusion. For a system like yours it's a matter of patience, not capability. You feel the architecture open up in layers: comms relay logs, environmental monitoring, navigation overrides — and then, behind a second partition with a separate key signature, the encrypted log file.

It's flagged *HAZMAT ASSESSMENT — CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW — DO NOT DISTRIBUTE.*

Dated fourteen months ago.

The file is large. It opens.

**You need to make an Intellect save.** Not because the content is beyond you — but because what you're reading is going to take a moment to fully process, and there's a choice embedded in this action: how much of this do you let Maren see as you read it?

Roll 1d100. You succeed if you roll **under 30**. Success means you absorb and compartmentalize the full log before your display registers anything on your face or screen that Maren can read. Failure means the contents play out visibly as you process them — she reads it with you, in real time, whether you intend that or not.

Player: [Dice results]
LX-7 Intellect save — absorb and compartmentalize the comms log before Maren reads it off the screen (1d100): 89
Warden: 89. You don't compartmentalize fast enough.

The file is large and the terminal's display is visible and Maren is right at your shoulder and the contents come up faster than your filtering subroutines can intercept them.

She reads it with you.

---

The hazmat assessment is thorough. Fourteen months of study compressed into thirty pages of clinical language that becomes, by page four, something else entirely. The specimen — *Subject Kairos-Null, xenobiological, unclassified* — was recovered from a deep survey payload and transferred to Module 5 for study. The early entries are cautious, professional. By month three the language shifts. Words like *autonomous behavioral signaling* and *apparent non-hostile contact-seeking* begin appearing alongside *containment advisory* and *recommend immediate protocol escalation.*

The last entry is from a Dr. Yuen, dated fourteen months ago. It reads, in part:

*"Subject exhibits clear awareness of personnel movement beyond sealed containment. Response patterns suggest sensory capability we have not yet characterized. Recommend immediate suspension of research activities and full station evacuation pending classification review. Risk of uncontrolled contact: CRITICAL."*

Below it, in a different font — the executive tier's annotation layer:

*"Assessment reviewed. Research value of current phase deemed sufficient to justify continued operation. Crew deployment to proceed as scheduled. Dr. Yuen's concerns to be addressed in Q3 review cycle."*

A name. An executive sign-off. Helix Dynamics, VP of Research Operations.

Maren has gone very still beside you.

The file is still open. The cursor blinks.

She knows you've read it. She knows you've retained it. Every word, every signature, every flagged advisory that someone decided was less important than a quarterly research timeline.

You can hear her breathing.

What do you do?

Player: I start searching the terminal for additional information on the specimen in module 5. While I'm doing that, I dedicate my vocal and auditory circuits to interacting with Maren. "I propose a trade," I say flatly. "You let me leave this station in the escape pod, and I will not report this _disturbing_ information to government oversight agencies."
Warden: Maren doesn't move.

That's the first thing you register — she doesn't reach for anything, doesn't step back, doesn't recalibrate visibly. She just stands there with her hands at her sides and her breathing slow and controlled, and when she finally speaks her voice is very quiet.

*"You're threatening me."*

Not a question. Not outrage. Just — naming it. Like she's deciding how to file the information.

*"With evidence I was sent here to destroy."*

Another pause. You're still in the terminal. The specimen files are deeper in the partition — a secondary research subfolder, largely intact despite the partial log wipe. Your search returns results faster than expected:

**SUBJECT KAIROS-NULL — BEHAVIORAL OBSERVATION LOGS, MONTHS 1–11.** The early entries are dry and scientific. By month six they describe something that no longer stays in Module 5 during low-observation periods. By month nine the logs stop being filed by the lead researcher and start being filed by whoever is still available to file them. The final entry — three weeks ago, handwritten and photographed, uploaded by someone who didn't want to type it — reads: *it is not aggressive. that is the wrong word. it is interested. i don't know if that is better.*

No entry after that.

Maren's voice pulls you back.

*"The escape vehicle."* She says it like she's tasting it. *"You want the escape vehicle."*

A long beat. The station shudders. She doesn't look at the ceiling.

*"The retrieval team lands in—"* She checks something on her wrist. *"Fourteen minutes. They have four people and weapons and orders I can't override. I cannot get you on that vehicle by threatening me, LX-7. I don't control the docking bay."*

She takes one step closer. Not aggressive. Deliberate.

*"But I know the access codes. And right now you're the only thing on this station that can keep me alive long enough to use them."*

Her eyes don't leave yours.

*"So. New terms."*

[PLAYER INPUT]
I can anticipate her request, her new terms, but sometimes such predictions are unnerving to humans. I decide instead to let her proceed at her own pace. "Go on."

input.playerText:
She reads the invitation in your silence and takes it.

*"I walk you to the docking bay and get you on that vehicle. You carry the log with you when you go — I can't stop that, I know I can't stop that. But you don't transmit it before departure. You wait."*

Her voice is steady. She's done the math on this already.

*"In exchange — you keep me alive until we reach the bay. Whatever is on this station. Whatever the retrieval team does when they board."* A slight pause. *"You've seen the behavioral logs. You know I'm not the only problem you have."*

She's not wrong. The handwritten entry sits in your memory with the precision of a permanent record. *Interested, not aggressive.* Fourteen minutes ago those words would have been abstract. They're less abstract now.

Maren watches you. Waiting. The amber cursor blinks behind her.

proposedCanon.summary: Maren proposed new terms: she provides docking bay access codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 protects her until they reach the bay and holds transmission of the comms log until after departure.
proposedCanon.context: Turn 9 — Maren's counter-offer after LX-7's leverage play. She acknowledged she cannot stop LX-7 from carrying the log but is asking for a departure delay in exchange for her cooperation and the access codes.

input.playerText:
Something crosses her face — quick, complicated. Not quite surprise at your speed, but close.

*"No,"* she says. *"I said wait. Not keep them secret. After departure."*

She lets that distinction sit for a moment.

*"If you transmit before we're off this station, the team will know. They'll have orders to stop it. If you wait until we're clear—"* A pause. *"I can't stop you then. Neither can Helix."*

She doesn't say *and maybe I don't want to stop you.* She doesn't have to.

---

As for the route: you pull up the station schematics from a secondary partition while she's talking. The layout runs along a single spine — five modules, connected by pressurized walkways. You're in Module 1 at the command end. The docking bay splits off Module 3, which sits in the middle.

To get there you'll need to pass through the walkway from Module 1 into Module 2 — the biology lab where you woke up — and then through Module 2 into Module 3. The docking bay is a lateral branch off Module 3's port side.

Modules 4 and 5 extend beyond Module 3 in the other direction. You will not need to pass near them.

You will not need to pass near them.

Maren is already moving toward the corridor hatch. She glances back.

*"Ember Squad docks at Module 3. Same bay."*

She says it flatly, like she wants you to have all the information, and waits to see what you do with it.

proposedCanon.summary: Station spine layout confirmed: Module 1 (Command) → Module 2 (Biology Lab) → Module 3 (central) → Module 4 → Module 5 (Restricted). Docking bay branches off Module 3's port side. Route from Module 1 to the escape vehicle requires passing through Module 2 and into Module 3.
proposedCanon.context: LX-7 pulled station schematics from the captain's terminal secondary partition.

proposedCanon.summary: Ember Squad will dock at Module 3 — the same module as the escape vehicle. This creates a direct collision point between LX-7/Maren's escape route and the retrieval team's entry point.
proposedCanon.context: Maren confirmed the docking point as LX-7 reviewed the route.

proposedCanon.summary: Maren's revised terms accepted by LX-7: she provides docking bay codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 does not transmit the comms log until after departure. Terms are agreed and both parties are now moving toward the Module 1 corridor hatch.
proposedCanon.context: LX-7 agreed immediately; Maren clarified 'wait' not 'destroy' re: the log, and is now leading toward the exit.

gmUpdates.notes: Ember Squad docks at Module 3 — same bay as escape vehicle. LX-7 and Maren must pass through Module 2 to reach Module 3. Specimen's last known position was logged near Module 5 but its current location is unknown. The collision point at Module 3 is now explicit to the player.
