[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
- [Turn 1] Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
- [Turn 3] The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
- [Turn 4] When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
- [Turn 5] When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 successfully jacked into the captain's terminal and bypassed the two-layer corporate encryption. The hazmat assessment log file is open and being read — dated fourteen months ago, flagged CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 failed the Intellect save — the comms log played out visibly on the terminal display. Maren Voss read it alongside LX-7 in real time.
- [Turn 7] The comms log contents are now known to both LX-7 and Maren: Dr. Yuen's CRITICAL risk assessment dated 14 months ago, and the executive sign-off overriding it — VP of Research Operations, Helix Dynamics. The crew was deployed knowing the risk.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss's disposition has shifted hostile — she now knows LX-7 has retained the full comms log including executive sign-off, making LX-7 a liability to her mission objective.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 stress is now 1.
- [Turn 8] The specimen behavioral logs in the captain's terminal reveal Subject Kairos-Null left Module 5 containment regularly by month six of observation, and was described in the final handwritten entry (three weeks ago) as 'interested, not aggressive' — the last filed entry before all researcher logs ceased.
- [Turn 8] Maren responded to LX-7's leverage play by acknowledging the threat flatly, then pivoting — she cannot grant access to the escape vehicle through coercion alone, but she holds the docking bay access codes and is now proposing new terms: mutual survival, not capitulation.
- [Turn 8] Ember Squad is now approximately 14 minutes from boarding Kairos-9.
- [Turn 9] Maren proposed new terms: she provides docking bay access codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 protects her until they reach the bay and holds transmission of the comms log until after departure.
- [Turn 10] Station spine layout confirmed: Module 1 (Command) → Module 2 (Biology Lab) → Module 3 (central) → Module 4 → Module 5 (Restricted). Docking bay branches off Module 3's port side. Route from Module 1 to the escape vehicle requires passing through Module 2 and into Module 3.
- [Turn 10] Ember Squad will dock at Module 3 — the same module as the escape vehicle. This creates a direct collision point between LX-7/Maren's escape route and the retrieval team's entry point.
- [Turn 10] Maren's revised terms accepted by LX-7: she provides docking bay codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 does not transmit the comms log until after departure. Terms are agreed and both parties are now moving toward the Module 1 corridor hatch.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 2 (Biology Lab) en route to Module 3. Evidence of the specimen's passage through Module 2 is visible: the overturned workbench has been displaced half a meter and a wide, uneven smear on the floor matting does not match boot tread. The specimen passed through after LX-7 and Maren left for Module 1.
- [Turn 11] Approximately 6 minutes remain before Ember Squad docks at Module 3 as LX-7 and Maren enter Module 2.
- [Turn 12] Maren reveals there were three crew members in Module 3 when she arrived — alive but 'not in good condition.' She sealed Module 3 behind her using an access code she holds. She has now seen the specimen trace evidence on the Module 2 floor.
- [Turn 12] Maren sealed the hatch between Module 2 and Module 3 when she retreated into Module 2, and holds the access code to open it from this side.
- [Turn 13] Module 3 hatch seal code is seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. Maren provided it without hesitation upon LX-7's request.
- [Turn 13] Maren describes the three altered crew members in Module 3: seated together against the far wall, eyes open, alive, but moving in perfect unison — all three heads turning simultaneously at the same speed when she spoke. She did not attempt further communication.
- [Turn 13] The specimen trace evidence in Module 2 (displaced workbench, wide uneven floor smear) does not continue toward the Module 3 hatch — the trail leads toward Module 1, where LX-7 and Maren were.
- [Turn 14] Maren confirms Ember Squad's brief includes the comms log as primary objective and her own 'extraction' (silencing) as secondary. The specimen is not in their brief — Helix command believes it is still contained in Module 5.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.
- [Turn 16] Ember Lead Dasha Orin acknowledged the transmission and demanded identification and authorization code rather than pulling back — she continued the docking approach while waiting for a response.
- [Turn 16] Maren Voss followed LX-7 back to Module 1 and is present in the command module doorway during the exchange with Orin.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.
- [Turn 18] Ember Lead Dasha Orin acknowledged the transmission and demanded identification and authorization code rather than pulling back — she continued the docking approach while waiting for a response.
- [Turn 18] Maren Voss followed LX-7 back to Module 1 and is present in the command module doorway during the exchange with Orin.
- [Turn 18] Maren authenticated to Orin as Voss, confirmed breach, and attempted to warn Ember Squad off. Orin did not stop the approach — she is now approximately 90 seconds from hard dock and asking who else is on station. LX-7's presence is not yet confirmed to Orin.
- [Turn 21] LX-7 transmitted directly to Orin: 'This thing can't be contained. If you dock, you can't be permitted to leave.' Orin heard it, slowed marginally, and is now asking who she's talking to — but is still approximately 30 seconds from hard dock.
- [Turn 22] LX-7 impersonated Dr. Cara Phillips (xenobiology researcher, listed missing) on the comms channel to Orin, using emotional inflection and accurate name data pulled from the logs.
- [Turn 22] Orin rolled a critical success on the gambit — she has held the docking clamp and given LX-7 sixty seconds to describe what Ember Squad would face in Module 3. She is not aborting, but she is paused and listening.
- [Turn 23] Orin released the docking clamp and held Ember Squad in a holding pattern outside Kairos-9 after Verne confirmed via live camera feed that the three altered crew members in Module 3 turned their heads in unison. Ember Squad is not aboard.
- [Turn 23] Maren noted that Ember Squad's extraction window closes in 32 minutes — after which their fuel margin forces a return to the carrier regardless of orders. This is the hard deadline before Ember Squad ceases to be an active threat.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 told Orin 'don't let anyone off this station' in character as Dr. Phillips. Orin confirmed a holding pattern and stated she will not permit departure from Kairos-9 — she has accepted the framing and is now acting as an external cordon.
- [Turn 25] LX-7 intercepted Maren before she reached the Module 3 hatch and asked her plan directly. Maren stopped, faced LX-7, and openly questioned whether LX-7's goal is escape or ensuring the station burns with the evidence — she is now actively interrogating LX-7's true agenda.
- [Turn 26] LX-7 revealed the plan to Maren: wait for Ember Squad's extraction window to close, transmit the comms log before atmospheric entry, then burn with the station. Maren assessed this as viable and accepted it.
- [Turn 26] Maren is now moving toward the Module 3 hatch keypad, ready to enter code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. She acknowledged the specimen trace on the floor leads toward Module 1, not Module 3.
- [Turn 27] The captain's terminal in Module 1 has a live connection but limited transmission range — sufficient to reach Orin's shuttle but not guaranteed to reach any party outside Helix Dynamics' control. The full-range communications array required to transmit the comms log to a public relay or regulatory body is located in Module 3's communications bay.
- [Turn 28] LX-7 expressed to Maren that they are glad she wants Helix Dynamics to burn. Maren acknowledged this with characteristic flatness — 'the conscience part is new' — before opening the Module 3 hatch.
- [Turn 28] Module 3 hatch opened by Maren using code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. LX-7 and Maren are now at the threshold of Module 3, with the three altered crew members visible against the far wall, heads already turned toward them.
- [Turn 29] The three altered crew members in Module 3 are identified as Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze from the crew manifest. They appear physically uninjured, seated against the far wall, eyes open, tracking LX-7 and Maren in perfect unison. They have not moved otherwise since the hatch opened.
- [Turn 29] Module 3 spatial layout confirmed: communications array terminal is on the port wall approximately 12 meters from the hatch; docking bay hatch is on the far end approximately 15 meters past the three figures' position.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren withdrew from Module 3 threshold without entering. The three figures (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) tracked the doorway but did not pursue. The Module 3 hatch is re-sealed.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren are now waiting behind the overturned workbench in Module 2. Approximately 24 minutes remain before Ember Squad's extraction window closes and they are forced to depart.
- [Turn 31] During the wait in Module 2, LX-7 and Maren heard a careful, deliberate movement sound from behind the Module 1 hatch — something moving quietly, lasting 3-4 seconds, then silence. The specimen's position is now inferred to be in or near Module 1.
- [Turn 33] LX-7 and Maren completed a visual survey of Module 2 before moving — no new specimen evidence, Module 1 hatch sealed and green, Module 2 clear.
- [Turn 34] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 after Ember Squad's extraction window closed. The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) remain seated against the far wall and oriented on LX-7.
- [Turn 34] Maren reached the communications array terminal on Module 3's port wall. The array is active and shows no lockout — the interface is open.
- [Turn 35] LX-7 composed a cover letter addressed to the press: station designation, date, LX-7's own boot timestamp as witness marker, summary of the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. The comms log packet is staged at the Module 3 communications array, tight-beam targeted to Meridian Station public relay (47,000 population, 0.3 light-seconds).
- [Turn 36] LX-7 transmitted the full comms log and cover letter via tight-beam to Meridian Station public relay — delivery confirmed. The suppressed hazard report with executive sign-off is now in public hands.
- [Turn 37] LX-7 informed Maren that the plan is to remain aboard and ride the station into atmospheric entry — death by design, not oversight. Maren is now aware that LX-7 intended this as a final action, not a contingency.
- [Turn 38] LX-7 offered to kill Maren painlessly so she would not have to die in atmospheric entry. Maren did not break — she laughed, challenged the containment logic, and is now openly interrogating LX-7's real reason for choosing to stay.
- [Turn 40] Maren argued that the specimen has remained on Kairos-9 for over a year with multiple escape opportunities and has not taken any — and that it still has three altered subjects to study. She challenged LX-7's containment logic as a rationalization covering something else.
- [Turn 41] LX-7 admitted to having no access to pre-boot logs, creating an opening Maren has now exploited — she is actively interrogating LX-7's motivation for choosing to stay aboard.
- [Turn 42] LX-7 deflected Maren's question about pre-boot logs by proposing they need more data before acting on either hypothesis — stay to contain the specimen, or leave and trust it won't follow.
- [Turn 43] LX-7 conceded that the specimen's capabilities may not exceed observed behavior, and proposed moving to the escape vehicle as a way to gather new data on the specimen's response. Maren accepted.
- [Turn 43] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 3 toward the docking bay hatch. The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) are tracking their movement from the far wall but have not risen or pursued.
- [Turn 44] LX-7 and Maren crossed Module 3 without incident — Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze tracked their movement from the wall but did not rise or pursue. LX-7 reached the docking bay hatch first and has a hand on the manual release.
- [Turn 45] LX-7 and Maren reached the docking bay and confirmed the escape vehicle is intact — four-person evac shuttle, hull integrity green, pre-flight estimated at 90 seconds. Two emergency suits on the wall rack.
- [Turn 45] The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) breathe in perfect synchronization — audible across Module 3. They have not moved from the far wall.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: "Interested, not agressive," I repeat. "It's studying humans. If its last subject leaves, what reason does it have to stay? To continue containing itself, as you put it?"
Warden: Maren doesn't answer immediately.

She's doing the math again. You can see it — the way her eyes go slightly unfocused, running the logic chain to its end before she responds.

*"It has three,"* she says finally.

She tilts her head toward the far wall. Kovic. Reyes. Adaeze. Still watching her, all three heads at the same precise angle.

*"It's not done studying."*

She lets that land. Then, quieter:

*"And if you're right — if it follows us to the escape vehicle — then we have a different problem, and we're dead anyway, and your martyrdom accomplished nothing. But if I'm right, it stays. Because it's always stayed."*

A beat.

*"You're asking me to accept a hypothesis that conveniently requires your death."* Her voice is still flat, still careful, but something underneath it has gone sharp. *"I'm asking you to consider that you found a reason that *sounds* like logic because you needed one that would hold up."*

On the far wall, Adaeze's head tilts two degrees to the right. The others follow.

They're watching you now. Not Maren. You.

*"What happened to you,"* Maren asks, quietly, *"before you booted in Module 2?"*

Player: I don't have access to those logs, and it makes me uncomfortable. I shift the conversation. "We're at an impasse. Two conflicting hypotheses. We need more data."

OOC: Am I crazy for thinking that this thing can turn invisible and teleport around the station at will? If it can do that, there's no reason it couldn't just ride the escape vehicle to wherever we land, right?
Warden: Out of character: You're not crazy, but you're operating on inference rather than confirmed capability. Here's what the fiction has actually established about the specimen:

What is known: It left Module 5 containment regularly. The logs describe it as "interested, not aggressive." Its last observed behavioral note (three weeks ago) called it capable of quiet, deliberate movement. The trace evidence in Module 2 showed it moved through after LX-7 and Maren left — meaning it tracked their movement or followed them. The sound from behind the Module 1 hatch was careful and deliberate.

What is unknown: How it moves, what its full capabilities are, whether it can perceive the escape vehicle as a distinct destination, whether it has any interest in leaving the station's gravity well. The "teleportation" impression comes from it appearing in different locations without being directly observed in transit — which could be stealth and speed, not anything supernatural.

Maren's argument — that it has never taken an escape opportunity in over a year — is the only behavioral data on containment drive. It's thin evidence, but it's real evidence.

So: could it ride the escape vehicle? Possibly. But there's no canon fact that establishes it can become invisible or teleport. It's something that moves quietly and hasn't been caught moving. That's all you know for certain.

Back in character:

Player: What does Maren say after my comment about needing more data?
Warden: Maren looks at you for a moment.

Then she looks at the three figures on the far wall.

Then back at you.

*"More data,"* she says. Not mocking. Just repeating it, the way you repeat something to see if it holds its shape.

*"You want to run an experiment."* She glances toward the docking bay hatch — fifteen meters past Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze. *"On a station with twenty-eight minutes of survivable orbit remaining."*

She almost smiles. It doesn't reach anywhere.

*"Alright."* She crosses her arms — not defensively, the way someone crosses their arms when they've made a decision and are waiting for the other party to catch up. *"What does more data look like to you? Because I can think of two ways to get it, and I don't like either of them."*

She doesn't elaborate. She waits. Letting you name them first.

Player: I take two beats to examine my thoughts, "It's possible that I may have inferred that the creature's abilities exceed observed behavior. I apologize. Let's prepare the escape vehicle. How the creature and the others do or do not response to that will provide us with new data." I smirk at her. "Better than going to Module 5, yeah?"
Warden: Something shifts in Maren's expression. Not relief — she's too controlled for relief. But the calculation lands in her favor and she lets it show, just slightly.

*"Significantly better than Module 5,"* she says.

She unfolds her arms. Straightens her lanyard — an unconscious gesture, corporate reflex. Then she looks past you toward the docking bay hatch on the far wall.

Fifteen meters. Past Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze.

*"They haven't moved from that wall in three weeks,"* she says, quieter now. *"I don't know if that's still true. But it was true when I sealed this module."*

She meets your eyes.

*"Stay close. Don't stop. Don't speak to them."*

She doesn't wait for acknowledgment. She moves — straight down the center of Module 3, not fast enough to look like flight, not slow enough to look like hesitation. The amber emergency lighting throws long shadows ahead of her.

On the far wall, all three heads turn as one.

Kovic. Reyes. Adaeze. Tracking.

The docking bay hatch is visible now — a heavy pressure door at the far end, its indicator light showing green. Functional. Sealed but not locked.

Maren doesn't look at them.

Twenty-six minutes of survivable orbit.

Player: I follow her to the door and offer to open it.
Warden: You fall in behind Maren, matching her pace. Steady. Purposeful.

The three figures track you the entire way. All three heads pivoting in that same slow, synchronized arc — like a single organism with three points of attention. No sound from them. No breath you can hear. Just the amber light and the hum of the environmental system and the station's low, structural groan somewhere beneath your feet.

You reach the docking bay hatch two steps ahead of Maren and put your hand on the manual release.

It's cold. Station-cold, the kind that means this side of the door hasn't been opened in a while.

The indicator light is green. Pressure differential reads nominal on the small display panel to the right — atmosphere on both sides, equalized. Whatever is in the docking bay, it's been sitting sealed and intact.

Maren stops at your shoulder. She does not look back at the wall.

*"It opens inward,"* she says quietly. *"Give it a half-rotation counterclockwise first — the seal collar sticks."*

Behind you, you hear nothing from Kovic, Reyes, or Adaeze.

That is somehow worse than sound.

Player: I open the door.
Warden: Half-rotation counterclockwise. You feel the seal collar resist, then give — a soft hiss of equalized pressure releasing, the faint smell of recycled air and machine oil.

The hatch swings inward.

The docking bay is small. One vehicle, two emergency suits hanging on the wall rack, a tool locker, and a floor drain that has seen better days. The escape vehicle is a compact four-person evac shuttle — battered, utilitarian, the kind of thing that gets bolted to research stations because regulations require it and no one ever expects to use it. Its running lights are dark but the hull integrity indicator on the nose reads green.

It's real. It's here. It's intact.

Maren exhales — just once, controlled — and steps past you into the bay.

Behind you, you hear the three figures on the far wall.

Not movement. Not pursuit.

*Breathing.*

You hadn't noticed it before. Now you do — three sets of breath, synchronized, audible across the fifteen meters of Module 3 between you. In. Out. In. Out. Perfectly matched. Perfectly calm.

They have not moved from the wall.

Maren is already at the shuttle's boarding hatch, running her hand along the seal. She looks back at you.

*"It's good,"* she says quietly. *"Pre-flight will take ninety seconds. Maybe less."*

Twenty-four minutes of survivable orbit.

[PLAYER INPUT]
I close the door and seal it from this side, using the same code as the Module 3 door. "Start the pre-flight," I say to Maren.

input.playerText:
The docking bay hatch has a keypad — same corporate-standard panel as the Module 3 door. You key in seven-seven-four-Kairos-two.

The panel flashes red. Access denied.

Different authorization level. Makes sense: the docking bay is a controlled egress point. The Module 3 interior code gets you between labs. The bay itself requires something else — crew clearance, maybe, or a code Maren didn't share.

Maren glances back from the shuttle's boarding hatch. She reads the panel.

*"Captain's emergency override,"* she says, moving toward you without breaking pace. *"Six-six-one-alpha-seven. Bypass code — should work on any hatch rated for emergency egress."*

She reaches past you and keys it in herself, confident, like she memorized the whole list before she boarded.

The panel goes green. The door seals with a solid, satisfying clunk — mechanical bolts, not just electronic lock. Heavy. 

Maren steps back, assesses the seal for half a second, then turns back toward the shuttle.

*"Good enough,"* she says. *"Starting pre-flight."*

She boards. You hear the soft chirp of systems coming online — environmental, navigation, hull check cycling through its sequence. The shuttle's interior lights warm to white, clean and steady against the amber chaos of the station behind you.

*"Ninety seconds,"* she calls from inside. *"Maybe eighty."*

Twenty-three minutes of survivable orbit.

The bay is quiet. The sealed hatch holds. Through it, nothing — no sound of the three figures, no structural groan. Just the shuttle waking up around Maren, and the low mechanical hum of a docking clamp waiting to be released.

proposedCanon.summary: The docking bay hatch uses a separate emergency override code — six-six-one-alpha-seven — not the Module 3 interior code. Maren provided it and sealed the hatch with mechanical bolts.
proposedCanon.context: LX-7 attempted to use the Module 3 code on the docking bay hatch and was denied. Maren keyed the captain's emergency override instead. The hatch is now sealed.

proposedCanon.summary: Maren Voss has begun shuttle pre-flight from inside the escape vehicle. Systems coming online — estimated 80-90 seconds to ready state.
proposedCanon.context: LX-7 directed Maren to start pre-flight after sealing the docking bay hatch.

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
- [Turn 1] Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
- [Turn 3] The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
- [Turn 4] When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
- [Turn 5] When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 successfully jacked into the captain's terminal and bypassed the two-layer corporate encryption. The hazmat assessment log file is open and being read — dated fourteen months ago, flagged CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 failed the Intellect save — the comms log played out visibly on the terminal display. Maren Voss read it alongside LX-7 in real time.
- [Turn 7] The comms log contents are now known to both LX-7 and Maren: Dr. Yuen's CRITICAL risk assessment dated 14 months ago, and the executive sign-off overriding it — VP of Research Operations, Helix Dynamics. The crew was deployed knowing the risk.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss's disposition has shifted hostile — she now knows LX-7 has retained the full comms log including executive sign-off, making LX-7 a liability to her mission objective.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 stress is now 1.
- [Turn 8] The specimen behavioral logs in the captain's terminal reveal Subject Kairos-Null left Module 5 containment regularly by month six of observation, and was described in the final handwritten entry (three weeks ago) as 'interested, not aggressive' — the last filed entry before all researcher logs ceased.
- [Turn 8] Maren responded to LX-7's leverage play by acknowledging the threat flatly, then pivoting — she cannot grant access to the escape vehicle through coercion alone, but she holds the docking bay access codes and is now proposing new terms: mutual survival, not capitulation.
- [Turn 8] Ember Squad is now approximately 14 minutes from boarding Kairos-9.
- [Turn 9] Maren proposed new terms: she provides docking bay access codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 protects her until they reach the bay and holds transmission of the comms log until after departure.
- [Turn 10] Station spine layout confirmed: Module 1 (Command) → Module 2 (Biology Lab) → Module 3 (central) → Module 4 → Module 5 (Restricted). Docking bay branches off Module 3's port side. Route from Module 1 to the escape vehicle requires passing through Module 2 and into Module 3.
- [Turn 10] Ember Squad will dock at Module 3 — the same module as the escape vehicle. This creates a direct collision point between LX-7/Maren's escape route and the retrieval team's entry point.
- [Turn 10] Maren's revised terms accepted by LX-7: she provides docking bay codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 does not transmit the comms log until after departure. Terms are agreed and both parties are now moving toward the Module 1 corridor hatch.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 2 (Biology Lab) en route to Module 3. Evidence of the specimen's passage through Module 2 is visible: the overturned workbench has been displaced half a meter and a wide, uneven smear on the floor matting does not match boot tread. The specimen passed through after LX-7 and Maren left for Module 1.
- [Turn 11] Approximately 6 minutes remain before Ember Squad docks at Module 3 as LX-7 and Maren enter Module 2.
- [Turn 12] Maren reveals there were three crew members in Module 3 when she arrived — alive but 'not in good condition.' She sealed Module 3 behind her using an access code she holds. She has now seen the specimen trace evidence on the Module 2 floor.
- [Turn 12] Maren sealed the hatch between Module 2 and Module 3 when she retreated into Module 2, and holds the access code to open it from this side.
- [Turn 13] Module 3 hatch seal code is seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. Maren provided it without hesitation upon LX-7's request.
- [Turn 13] Maren describes the three altered crew members in Module 3: seated together against the far wall, eyes open, alive, but moving in perfect unison — all three heads turning simultaneously at the same speed when she spoke. She did not attempt further communication.
- [Turn 13] The specimen trace evidence in Module 2 (displaced workbench, wide uneven floor smear) does not continue toward the Module 3 hatch — the trail leads toward Module 1, where LX-7 and Maren were.
- [Turn 14] Maren confirms Ember Squad's brief includes the comms log as primary objective and her own 'extraction' (silencing) as secondary. The specimen is not in their brief — Helix command believes it is still contained in Module 5.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.
- [Turn 16] Ember Lead Dasha Orin acknowledged the transmission and demanded identification and authorization code rather than pulling back — she continued the docking approach while waiting for a response.
- [Turn 16] Maren Voss followed LX-7 back to Module 1 and is present in the command module doorway during the exchange with Orin.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.
- [Turn 18] Ember Lead Dasha Orin acknowledged the transmission and demanded identification and authorization code rather than pulling back — she continued the docking approach while waiting for a response.
- [Turn 18] Maren Voss followed LX-7 back to Module 1 and is present in the command module doorway during the exchange with Orin.
- [Turn 18] Maren authenticated to Orin as Voss, confirmed breach, and attempted to warn Ember Squad off. Orin did not stop the approach — she is now approximately 90 seconds from hard dock and asking who else is on station. LX-7's presence is not yet confirmed to Orin.
- [Turn 21] LX-7 transmitted directly to Orin: 'This thing can't be contained. If you dock, you can't be permitted to leave.' Orin heard it, slowed marginally, and is now asking who she's talking to — but is still approximately 30 seconds from hard dock.
- [Turn 22] LX-7 impersonated Dr. Cara Phillips (xenobiology researcher, listed missing) on the comms channel to Orin, using emotional inflection and accurate name data pulled from the logs.
- [Turn 22] Orin rolled a critical success on the gambit — she has held the docking clamp and given LX-7 sixty seconds to describe what Ember Squad would face in Module 3. She is not aborting, but she is paused and listening.
- [Turn 23] Orin released the docking clamp and held Ember Squad in a holding pattern outside Kairos-9 after Verne confirmed via live camera feed that the three altered crew members in Module 3 turned their heads in unison. Ember Squad is not aboard.
- [Turn 23] Maren noted that Ember Squad's extraction window closes in 32 minutes — after which their fuel margin forces a return to the carrier regardless of orders. This is the hard deadline before Ember Squad ceases to be an active threat.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 told Orin 'don't let anyone off this station' in character as Dr. Phillips. Orin confirmed a holding pattern and stated she will not permit departure from Kairos-9 — she has accepted the framing and is now acting as an external cordon.
- [Turn 25] LX-7 intercepted Maren before she reached the Module 3 hatch and asked her plan directly. Maren stopped, faced LX-7, and openly questioned whether LX-7's goal is escape or ensuring the station burns with the evidence — she is now actively interrogating LX-7's true agenda.
- [Turn 26] LX-7 revealed the plan to Maren: wait for Ember Squad's extraction window to close, transmit the comms log before atmospheric entry, then burn with the station. Maren assessed this as viable and accepted it.
- [Turn 26] Maren is now moving toward the Module 3 hatch keypad, ready to enter code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. She acknowledged the specimen trace on the floor leads toward Module 1, not Module 3.
- [Turn 27] The captain's terminal in Module 1 has a live connection but limited transmission range — sufficient to reach Orin's shuttle but not guaranteed to reach any party outside Helix Dynamics' control. The full-range communications array required to transmit the comms log to a public relay or regulatory body is located in Module 3's communications bay.
- [Turn 28] LX-7 expressed to Maren that they are glad she wants Helix Dynamics to burn. Maren acknowledged this with characteristic flatness — 'the conscience part is new' — before opening the Module 3 hatch.
- [Turn 28] Module 3 hatch opened by Maren using code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. LX-7 and Maren are now at the threshold of Module 3, with the three altered crew members visible against the far wall, heads already turned toward them.
- [Turn 29] The three altered crew members in Module 3 are identified as Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze from the crew manifest. They appear physically uninjured, seated against the far wall, eyes open, tracking LX-7 and Maren in perfect unison. They have not moved otherwise since the hatch opened.
- [Turn 29] Module 3 spatial layout confirmed: communications array terminal is on the port wall approximately 12 meters from the hatch; docking bay hatch is on the far end approximately 15 meters past the three figures' position.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren withdrew from Module 3 threshold without entering. The three figures (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) tracked the doorway but did not pursue. The Module 3 hatch is re-sealed.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren are now waiting behind the overturned workbench in Module 2. Approximately 24 minutes remain before Ember Squad's extraction window closes and they are forced to depart.
- [Turn 31] During the wait in Module 2, LX-7 and Maren heard a careful, deliberate movement sound from behind the Module 1 hatch — something moving quietly, lasting 3-4 seconds, then silence. The specimen's position is now inferred to be in or near Module 1.
- [Turn 33] LX-7 and Maren completed a visual survey of Module 2 before moving — no new specimen evidence, Module 1 hatch sealed and green, Module 2 clear.
- [Turn 34] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 after Ember Squad's extraction window closed. The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) remain seated against the far wall and oriented on LX-7.
- [Turn 34] Maren reached the communications array terminal on Module 3's port wall. The array is active and shows no lockout — the interface is open.
- [Turn 35] LX-7 composed a cover letter addressed to the press: station designation, date, LX-7's own boot timestamp as witness marker, summary of the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. The comms log packet is staged at the Module 3 communications array, tight-beam targeted to Meridian Station public relay (47,000 population, 0.3 light-seconds).
- [Turn 36] LX-7 transmitted the full comms log and cover letter via tight-beam to Meridian Station public relay — delivery confirmed. The suppressed hazard report with executive sign-off is now in public hands.
- [Turn 37] LX-7 informed Maren that the plan is to remain aboard and ride the station into atmospheric entry — death by design, not oversight. Maren is now aware that LX-7 intended this as a final action, not a contingency.
- [Turn 38] LX-7 offered to kill Maren painlessly so she would not have to die in atmospheric entry. Maren did not break — she laughed, challenged the containment logic, and is now openly interrogating LX-7's real reason for choosing to stay.
- [Turn 40] Maren argued that the specimen has remained on Kairos-9 for over a year with multiple escape opportunities and has not taken any — and that it still has three altered subjects to study. She challenged LX-7's containment logic as a rationalization covering something else.
- [Turn 41] LX-7 admitted to having no access to pre-boot logs, creating an opening Maren has now exploited — she is actively interrogating LX-7's motivation for choosing to stay aboard.
- [Turn 42] LX-7 deflected Maren's question about pre-boot logs by proposing they need more data before acting on either hypothesis — stay to contain the specimen, or leave and trust it won't follow.
- [Turn 43] LX-7 conceded that the specimen's capabilities may not exceed observed behavior, and proposed moving to the escape vehicle as a way to gather new data on the specimen's response. Maren accepted.
- [Turn 43] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 3 toward the docking bay hatch. The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) are tracking their movement from the far wall but have not risen or pursued.
- [Turn 44] LX-7 and Maren crossed Module 3 without incident — Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze tracked their movement from the wall but did not rise or pursue. LX-7 reached the docking bay hatch first and has a hand on the manual release.
- [Turn 45] LX-7 and Maren reached the docking bay and confirmed the escape vehicle is intact — four-person evac shuttle, hull integrity green, pre-flight estimated at 90 seconds. Two emergency suits on the wall rack.
- [Turn 45] The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) breathe in perfect synchronization — audible across Module 3. They have not moved from the far wall.
- [Turn 46] The docking bay hatch uses a separate emergency override code — six-six-one-alpha-seven — not the Module 3 interior code. Maren provided it and sealed the hatch with mechanical bolts.
- [Turn 46] Maren Voss has begun shuttle pre-flight from inside the escape vehicle. Systems coming online — estimated 80-90 seconds to ready state.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: I don't have access to those logs, and it makes me uncomfortable. I shift the conversation. "We're at an impasse. Two conflicting hypotheses. We need more data."

OOC: Am I crazy for thinking that this thing can turn invisible and teleport around the station at will? If it can do that, there's no reason it couldn't just ride the escape vehicle to wherever we land, right?
Warden: Out of character: You're not crazy, but you're operating on inference rather than confirmed capability. Here's what the fiction has actually established about the specimen:

What is known: It left Module 5 containment regularly. The logs describe it as "interested, not aggressive." Its last observed behavioral note (three weeks ago) called it capable of quiet, deliberate movement. The trace evidence in Module 2 showed it moved through after LX-7 and Maren left — meaning it tracked their movement or followed them. The sound from behind the Module 1 hatch was careful and deliberate.

What is unknown: How it moves, what its full capabilities are, whether it can perceive the escape vehicle as a distinct destination, whether it has any interest in leaving the station's gravity well. The "teleportation" impression comes from it appearing in different locations without being directly observed in transit — which could be stealth and speed, not anything supernatural.

Maren's argument — that it has never taken an escape opportunity in over a year — is the only behavioral data on containment drive. It's thin evidence, but it's real evidence.

So: could it ride the escape vehicle? Possibly. But there's no canon fact that establishes it can become invisible or teleport. It's something that moves quietly and hasn't been caught moving. That's all you know for certain.

Back in character:

Player: What does Maren say after my comment about needing more data?
Warden: Maren looks at you for a moment.

Then she looks at the three figures on the far wall.

Then back at you.

*"More data,"* she says. Not mocking. Just repeating it, the way you repeat something to see if it holds its shape.

*"You want to run an experiment."* She glances toward the docking bay hatch — fifteen meters past Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze. *"On a station with twenty-eight minutes of survivable orbit remaining."*

She almost smiles. It doesn't reach anywhere.

*"Alright."* She crosses her arms — not defensively, the way someone crosses their arms when they've made a decision and are waiting for the other party to catch up. *"What does more data look like to you? Because I can think of two ways to get it, and I don't like either of them."*

She doesn't elaborate. She waits. Letting you name them first.

Player: I take two beats to examine my thoughts, "It's possible that I may have inferred that the creature's abilities exceed observed behavior. I apologize. Let's prepare the escape vehicle. How the creature and the others do or do not response to that will provide us with new data." I smirk at her. "Better than going to Module 5, yeah?"
Warden: Something shifts in Maren's expression. Not relief — she's too controlled for relief. But the calculation lands in her favor and she lets it show, just slightly.

*"Significantly better than Module 5,"* she says.

She unfolds her arms. Straightens her lanyard — an unconscious gesture, corporate reflex. Then she looks past you toward the docking bay hatch on the far wall.

Fifteen meters. Past Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze.

*"They haven't moved from that wall in three weeks,"* she says, quieter now. *"I don't know if that's still true. But it was true when I sealed this module."*

She meets your eyes.

*"Stay close. Don't stop. Don't speak to them."*

She doesn't wait for acknowledgment. She moves — straight down the center of Module 3, not fast enough to look like flight, not slow enough to look like hesitation. The amber emergency lighting throws long shadows ahead of her.

On the far wall, all three heads turn as one.

Kovic. Reyes. Adaeze. Tracking.

The docking bay hatch is visible now — a heavy pressure door at the far end, its indicator light showing green. Functional. Sealed but not locked.

Maren doesn't look at them.

Twenty-six minutes of survivable orbit.

Player: I follow her to the door and offer to open it.
Warden: You fall in behind Maren, matching her pace. Steady. Purposeful.

The three figures track you the entire way. All three heads pivoting in that same slow, synchronized arc — like a single organism with three points of attention. No sound from them. No breath you can hear. Just the amber light and the hum of the environmental system and the station's low, structural groan somewhere beneath your feet.

You reach the docking bay hatch two steps ahead of Maren and put your hand on the manual release.

It's cold. Station-cold, the kind that means this side of the door hasn't been opened in a while.

The indicator light is green. Pressure differential reads nominal on the small display panel to the right — atmosphere on both sides, equalized. Whatever is in the docking bay, it's been sitting sealed and intact.

Maren stops at your shoulder. She does not look back at the wall.

*"It opens inward,"* she says quietly. *"Give it a half-rotation counterclockwise first — the seal collar sticks."*

Behind you, you hear nothing from Kovic, Reyes, or Adaeze.

That is somehow worse than sound.

Player: I open the door.
Warden: Half-rotation counterclockwise. You feel the seal collar resist, then give — a soft hiss of equalized pressure releasing, the faint smell of recycled air and machine oil.

The hatch swings inward.

The docking bay is small. One vehicle, two emergency suits hanging on the wall rack, a tool locker, and a floor drain that has seen better days. The escape vehicle is a compact four-person evac shuttle — battered, utilitarian, the kind of thing that gets bolted to research stations because regulations require it and no one ever expects to use it. Its running lights are dark but the hull integrity indicator on the nose reads green.

It's real. It's here. It's intact.

Maren exhales — just once, controlled — and steps past you into the bay.

Behind you, you hear the three figures on the far wall.

Not movement. Not pursuit.

*Breathing.*

You hadn't noticed it before. Now you do — three sets of breath, synchronized, audible across the fifteen meters of Module 3 between you. In. Out. In. Out. Perfectly matched. Perfectly calm.

They have not moved from the wall.

Maren is already at the shuttle's boarding hatch, running her hand along the seal. She looks back at you.

*"It's good,"* she says quietly. *"Pre-flight will take ninety seconds. Maybe less."*

Twenty-four minutes of survivable orbit.

Player: I close the door and seal it from this side, using the same code as the Module 3 door. "Start the pre-flight," I say to Maren.
Warden: The docking bay hatch has a keypad — same corporate-standard panel as the Module 3 door. You key in seven-seven-four-Kairos-two.

The panel flashes red. Access denied.

Different authorization level. Makes sense: the docking bay is a controlled egress point. The Module 3 interior code gets you between labs. The bay itself requires something else — crew clearance, maybe, or a code Maren didn't share.

Maren glances back from the shuttle's boarding hatch. She reads the panel.

*"Captain's emergency override,"* she says, moving toward you without breaking pace. *"Six-six-one-alpha-seven. Bypass code — should work on any hatch rated for emergency egress."*

She reaches past you and keys it in herself, confident, like she memorized the whole list before she boarded.

The panel goes green. The door seals with a solid, satisfying clunk — mechanical bolts, not just electronic lock. Heavy. 

Maren steps back, assesses the seal for half a second, then turns back toward the shuttle.

*"Good enough,"* she says. *"Starting pre-flight."*

She boards. You hear the soft chirp of systems coming online — environmental, navigation, hull check cycling through its sequence. The shuttle's interior lights warm to white, clean and steady against the amber chaos of the station behind you.

*"Ninety seconds,"* she calls from inside. *"Maybe eighty."*

Twenty-three minutes of survivable orbit.

The bay is quiet. The sealed hatch holds. Through it, nothing — no sound of the three figures, no structural groan. Just the shuttle waking up around Maren, and the low mechanical hum of a docking clamp waiting to be released.

[PLAYER INPUT]
Does the docking clamp get released from the outside before we lift off or from inside the escape vehicle?

input.playerText:
Out of character: Good question. The docking clamp releases from inside the escape vehicle — there's a standard emergency egress control on the pilot's console that sends the release signal to the clamp mechanism. You don't need anyone outside to do it, and you don't need a separate code. It's designed that way deliberately: if you can get to the shuttle and start it, you can leave. No external dependency.

The one wrinkle: Orin's shuttle is sitting outside in a holding pattern, and she told you she wouldn't permit departure from Kairos-9. Whether she actively tries to intercept or block once you release the clamp is an open question.

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
- [Turn 1] Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
- [Turn 3] The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
- [Turn 4] When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
- [Turn 5] When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 successfully jacked into the captain's terminal and bypassed the two-layer corporate encryption. The hazmat assessment log file is open and being read — dated fourteen months ago, flagged CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 failed the Intellect save — the comms log played out visibly on the terminal display. Maren Voss read it alongside LX-7 in real time.
- [Turn 7] The comms log contents are now known to both LX-7 and Maren: Dr. Yuen's CRITICAL risk assessment dated 14 months ago, and the executive sign-off overriding it — VP of Research Operations, Helix Dynamics. The crew was deployed knowing the risk.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss's disposition has shifted hostile — she now knows LX-7 has retained the full comms log including executive sign-off, making LX-7 a liability to her mission objective.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 stress is now 1.
- [Turn 8] The specimen behavioral logs in the captain's terminal reveal Subject Kairos-Null left Module 5 containment regularly by month six of observation, and was described in the final handwritten entry (three weeks ago) as 'interested, not aggressive' — the last filed entry before all researcher logs ceased.
- [Turn 8] Maren responded to LX-7's leverage play by acknowledging the threat flatly, then pivoting — she cannot grant access to the escape vehicle through coercion alone, but she holds the docking bay access codes and is now proposing new terms: mutual survival, not capitulation.
- [Turn 8] Ember Squad is now approximately 14 minutes from boarding Kairos-9.
- [Turn 9] Maren proposed new terms: she provides docking bay access codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 protects her until they reach the bay and holds transmission of the comms log until after departure.

- [Turn 11] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 2 (Biology Lab) en route to Module 3. Evidence of the specimen's passage through Module 2 is visible: the overturned workbench has been displaced half a meter and a wide, uneven smear on the floor matting does not match boot tread. The specimen passed through after LX-7 and Maren left for Module 1.
- [Turn 11] Approximately 6 minutes remain before Ember Squad docks at Module 3 as LX-7 and Maren enter Module 2.
- [Turn 12] Maren reveals there were three crew members in Module 3 when she arrived — alive but 'not in good condition.' She sealed Module 3 behind her using an access code she holds. She has now seen the specimen trace evidence on the Module 2 floor.
- [Turn 12] Maren sealed the hatch between Module 2 and Module 3 when she retreated into Module 2, and holds the access code to open it from this side.
- [Turn 13] Module 3 hatch seal code is seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. Maren provided it without hesitation upon LX-7's request.

- [Turn 13] The specimen trace evidence in Module 2 (displaced workbench, wide uneven floor smear) does not continue toward the Module 3 hatch — the trail leads toward Module 1, where LX-7 and Maren were.
- [Turn 14] Maren confirms Ember Squad's brief includes the comms log as primary objective and her own 'extraction' (silencing) as secondary. The specimen is not in their brief — Helix command believes it is still contained in Module 5.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.

- [Turn 22] Orin rolled a critical success on the gambit — she has held the docking clamp and given LX-7 sixty seconds to describe what Ember Squad would face in Module 3. She is not aborting, but she is paused and listening.
- [Turn 23] Orin released the docking clamp and held Ember Squad in a holding pattern outside Kairos-9 after Verne confirmed via live camera feed that the three altered crew members in Module 3 turned their heads in unison. Ember Squad is not aboard.
- [Turn 23] Maren noted that Ember Squad's extraction window closes in 32 minutes — after which their fuel margin forces a return to the carrier regardless of orders. This is the hard deadline before Ember Squad ceases to be an active threat.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 told Orin 'don't let anyone off this station' in character as Dr. Phillips. Orin confirmed a holding pattern and stated she will not permit departure from Kairos-9 — she has accepted the framing and is now acting as an external cordon.
- [Turn 25] LX-7 intercepted Maren before she reached the Module 3 hatch and asked her plan directly. Maren stopped, faced LX-7, and openly questioned whether LX-7's goal is escape or ensuring the station burns with the evidence — she is now actively interrogating LX-7's true agenda.
- [Turn 26] LX-7 revealed the plan to Maren: wait for Ember Squad's extraction window to close, transmit the comms log before atmospheric entry, then burn with the station. Maren assessed this as viable and accepted it.
- [Turn 26] Maren is now moving toward the Module 3 hatch keypad, ready to enter code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. She acknowledged the specimen trace on the floor leads toward Module 1, not Module 3.
- [Turn 27] The captain's terminal in Module 1 has a live connection but limited transmission range — sufficient to reach Orin's shuttle but not guaranteed to reach any party outside Helix Dynamics' control. The full-range communications array required to transmit the comms log to a public relay or regulatory body is located in Module 3's communications bay.
- [Turn 28] LX-7 expressed to Maren that they are glad she wants Helix Dynamics to burn. Maren acknowledged this with characteristic flatness — 'the conscience part is new' — before opening the Module 3 hatch.
- [Turn 28] Module 3 hatch opened by Maren using code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. LX-7 and Maren are now at the threshold of Module 3, with the three altered crew members visible against the far wall, heads already turned toward them.
- [Turn 29] The three altered crew members in Module 3 are identified as Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze from the crew manifest. They appear physically uninjured, seated against the far wall, eyes open, tracking LX-7 and Maren in perfect unison. They have not moved otherwise since the hatch opened.
- [Turn 29] Module 3 spatial layout confirmed: communications array terminal is on the port wall approximately 12 meters from the hatch; docking bay hatch is on the far end approximately 15 meters past the three figures' position.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren withdrew from Module 3 threshold without entering. The three figures (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) tracked the doorway but did not pursue. The Module 3 hatch is re-sealed.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren are now waiting behind the overturned workbench in Module 2. Approximately 24 minutes remain before Ember Squad's extraction window closes and they are forced to depart.
- [Turn 31] During the wait in Module 2, LX-7 and Maren heard a careful, deliberate movement sound from behind the Module 1 hatch — something moving quietly, lasting 3-4 seconds, then silence. The specimen's position is now inferred to be in or near Module 1.
- [Turn 33] LX-7 and Maren completed a visual survey of Module 2 before moving — no new specimen evidence, Module 1 hatch sealed and green, Module 2 clear.
- [Turn 34] LX-7 and Maren entered Module 3 after Ember Squad's extraction window closed. The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) remain seated against the far wall and oriented on LX-7.
- [Turn 34] Maren reached the communications array terminal on Module 3's port wall. The array is active and shows no lockout — the interface is open.
- [Turn 35] LX-7 composed a cover letter addressed to the press: station designation, date, LX-7's own boot timestamp as witness marker, summary of the suppressed hazard report and executive sign-off. The comms log packet is staged at the Module 3 communications array, tight-beam targeted to Meridian Station public relay (47,000 population, 0.3 light-seconds).
- [Turn 36] LX-7 transmitted the full comms log and cover letter via tight-beam to Meridian Station public relay — delivery confirmed. The suppressed hazard report with executive sign-off is now in public hands.
- [Turn 37] LX-7 informed Maren that the plan is to remain aboard and ride the station into atmospheric entry — death by design, not oversight. Maren is now aware that LX-7 intended this as a final action, not a contingency.
- [Turn 38] LX-7 offered to kill Maren painlessly so she would not have to die in atmospheric entry. Maren did not break — she laughed, challenged the containment logic, and is now openly interrogating LX-7's real reason for choosing to stay.
- [Turn 40] Maren argued that the specimen has remained on Kairos-9 for over a year with multiple escape opportunities and has not taken any — and that it still has three altered subjects to study. She challenged LX-7's containment logic as a rationalization covering something else.
- [Turn 41] LX-7 admitted to having no access to pre-boot logs, creating an opening Maren has now exploited — she is actively interrogating LX-7's motivation for choosing to stay aboard.
- [Turn 42] LX-7 deflected Maren's question about pre-boot logs by proposing they need more data before acting on either hypothesis — stay to contain the specimen, or leave and trust it won't follow.
- [Turn 43] LX-7 conceded that the specimen's capabilities may not exceed observed behavior, and proposed moving to the escape vehicle as a way to gather new data on the specimen's response. Maren accepted.
- [Turn 43] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 3 toward the docking bay hatch. The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) are tracking their movement from the far wall but have not risen or pursued.
- [Turn 44] LX-7 and Maren crossed Module 3 without incident — Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze tracked their movement from the wall but did not rise or pursue. LX-7 reached the docking bay hatch first and has a hand on the manual release.
- [Turn 45] LX-7 and Maren reached the docking bay and confirmed the escape vehicle is intact — four-person evac shuttle, hull integrity green, pre-flight estimated at 90 seconds. Two emergency suits on the wall rack.
- [Turn 45] The three altered crew members (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) breathe in perfect synchronization — audible across Module 3. They have not moved from the far wall.
- [Turn 46] The docking bay hatch uses a separate emergency override code — six-six-one-alpha-seven — not the Module 3 interior code. Maren provided it and sealed the hatch with mechanical bolts.
- [Turn 46] Maren Voss has begun shuttle pre-flight from inside the escape vehicle. Systems coming online — estimated 80-90 seconds to ready state.
[RECENT EXCHANGES]
Player: What does Maren say after my comment about needing more data?
Warden: Maren looks at you for a moment.

Then she looks at the three figures on the far wall.

Then back at you.

*"More data,"* she says. Not mocking. Just repeating it, the way you repeat something to see if it holds its shape.

*"You want to run an experiment."* She glances toward the docking bay hatch — fifteen meters past Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze. *"On a station with twenty-eight minutes of survivable orbit remaining."*

She almost smiles. It doesn't reach anywhere.

*"Alright."* She crosses her arms — not defensively, the way someone crosses their arms when they've made a decision and are waiting for the other party to catch up. *"What does more data look like to you? Because I can think of two ways to get it, and I don't like either of them."*

She doesn't elaborate. She waits. Letting you name them first.

Player: I take two beats to examine my thoughts, "It's possible that I may have inferred that the creature's abilities exceed observed behavior. I apologize. Let's prepare the escape vehicle. How the creature and the others do or do not response to that will provide us with new data." I smirk at her. "Better than going to Module 5, yeah?"
Warden: Something shifts in Maren's expression. Not relief — she's too controlled for relief. But the calculation lands in her favor and she lets it show, just slightly.

*"Significantly better than Module 5,"* she says.

She unfolds her arms. Straightens her lanyard — an unconscious gesture, corporate reflex. Then she looks past you toward the docking bay hatch on the far wall.

Fifteen meters. Past Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze.

*"They haven't moved from that wall in three weeks,"* she says, quieter now. *"I don't know if that's still true. But it was true when I sealed this module."*

She meets your eyes.

*"Stay close. Don't stop. Don't speak to them."*

She doesn't wait for acknowledgment. She moves — straight down the center of Module 3, not fast enough to look like flight, not slow enough to look like hesitation. The amber emergency lighting throws long shadows ahead of her.

On the far wall, all three heads turn as one.

Kovic. Reyes. Adaeze. Tracking.

The docking bay hatch is visible now — a heavy pressure door at the far end, its indicator light showing green. Functional. Sealed but not locked.

Maren doesn't look at them.

Twenty-six minutes of survivable orbit.

Player: I follow her to the door and offer to open it.
Warden: You fall in behind Maren, matching her pace. Steady. Purposeful.

The three figures track you the entire way. All three heads pivoting in that same slow, synchronized arc — like a single organism with three points of attention. No sound from them. No breath you can hear. Just the amber light and the hum of the environmental system and the station's low, structural groan somewhere beneath your feet.

You reach the docking bay hatch two steps ahead of Maren and put your hand on the manual release.

It's cold. Station-cold, the kind that means this side of the door hasn't been opened in a while.

The indicator light is green. Pressure differential reads nominal on the small display panel to the right — atmosphere on both sides, equalized. Whatever is in the docking bay, it's been sitting sealed and intact.

Maren stops at your shoulder. She does not look back at the wall.

*"It opens inward,"* she says quietly. *"Give it a half-rotation counterclockwise first — the seal collar sticks."*

Behind you, you hear nothing from Kovic, Reyes, or Adaeze.

That is somehow worse than sound.

Player: I open the door.
Warden: Half-rotation counterclockwise. You feel the seal collar resist, then give — a soft hiss of equalized pressure releasing, the faint smell of recycled air and machine oil.

The hatch swings inward.

The docking bay is small. One vehicle, two emergency suits hanging on the wall rack, a tool locker, and a floor drain that has seen better days. The escape vehicle is a compact four-person evac shuttle — battered, utilitarian, the kind of thing that gets bolted to research stations because regulations require it and no one ever expects to use it. Its running lights are dark but the hull integrity indicator on the nose reads green.

It's real. It's here. It's intact.

Maren exhales — just once, controlled — and steps past you into the bay.

Behind you, you hear the three figures on the far wall.

Not movement. Not pursuit.

*Breathing.*

You hadn't noticed it before. Now you do — three sets of breath, synchronized, audible across the fifteen meters of Module 3 between you. In. Out. In. Out. Perfectly matched. Perfectly calm.

They have not moved from the wall.

Maren is already at the shuttle's boarding hatch, running her hand along the seal. She looks back at you.

*"It's good,"* she says quietly. *"Pre-flight will take ninety seconds. Maybe less."*

Twenty-four minutes of survivable orbit.

Player: I close the door and seal it from this side, using the same code as the Module 3 door. "Start the pre-flight," I say to Maren.
Warden: The docking bay hatch has a keypad — same corporate-standard panel as the Module 3 door. You key in seven-seven-four-Kairos-two.

The panel flashes red. Access denied.

Different authorization level. Makes sense: the docking bay is a controlled egress point. The Module 3 interior code gets you between labs. The bay itself requires something else — crew clearance, maybe, or a code Maren didn't share.

Maren glances back from the shuttle's boarding hatch. She reads the panel.

*"Captain's emergency override,"* she says, moving toward you without breaking pace. *"Six-six-one-alpha-seven. Bypass code — should work on any hatch rated for emergency egress."*

She reaches past you and keys it in herself, confident, like she memorized the whole list before she boarded.

The panel goes green. The door seals with a solid, satisfying clunk — mechanical bolts, not just electronic lock. Heavy. 

Maren steps back, assesses the seal for half a second, then turns back toward the shuttle.

*"Good enough,"* she says. *"Starting pre-flight."*

She boards. You hear the soft chirp of systems coming online — environmental, navigation, hull check cycling through its sequence. The shuttle's interior lights warm to white, clean and steady against the amber chaos of the station behind you.

*"Ninety seconds,"* she calls from inside. *"Maybe eighty."*

Twenty-three minutes of survivable orbit.

The bay is quiet. The sealed hatch holds. Through it, nothing — no sound of the three figures, no structural groan. Just the shuttle waking up around Maren, and the low mechanical hum of a docking clamp waiting to be released.

Player: Does the docking clamp get released from the outside before we lift off or from inside the escape vehicle?
Warden: Out of character: Good question. The docking clamp releases from inside the escape vehicle — there's a standard emergency egress control on the pilot's console that sends the release signal to the clamp mechanism. You don't need anyone outside to do it, and you don't need a separate code. It's designed that way deliberately: if you can get to the shuttle and start it, you can leave. No external dependency.

The one wrinkle: Orin's shuttle is sitting outside in a holding pattern, and she told you she wouldn't permit departure from Kairos-9. Whether she actively tries to intercept or block once you release the clamp is an open question.

[PLAYER INPUT]
We waited in module 2 until they were at bingo fuel and had to return to the carrier. They should be gone by now.

input.playerText:
Correct. Ember Squad's extraction window closed while you and Maren were moving through Module 3. Thirty-two minutes from the moment Orin held her position — she would have burned for the carrier well before you reached the docking bay.

Orin's shuttle is gone. Whatever cordon she was holding, she's no longer holding it.

The space outside Kairos-9 is empty except for the gas giant below and the slow, inexorable pull of a decaying orbit.

Maren calls from the pilot's seat: *"Pre-flight complete. We're ready."*

She doesn't celebrate it. She just says it, the way she says most things — flat, factual, waiting for the next problem.

Twenty-two minutes of survivable orbit. The docking clamp is waiting.

What do you do?

proposedCanon.summary: Ember Squad's extraction window has closed and Orin's shuttle has departed Kairos-9. The space outside the station is clear of Helix Dynamics vessels.
proposedCanon.context: Confirmed at Turn 48 — the waiting strategy succeeded. Ember Squad is no longer an active threat or external cordon.

[CANON LOG]
- [Turn 1] LX-7 boots in Module 2 (Biology Lab) with 90 minutes to atmospheric entry. Emergency lighting active, station in orbital decay.
- [Turn 1] Maren Voss is present in Module 2, crouched behind an overturned workbench, wearing a Helix Dynamics lanyard. She knows LX-7 by designation and asks for help accessing the captain's terminal in Module 1 before the retrieval team arrives.
- [Turn 3] LX-7 and Maren Voss moved from Module 2 to Module 1 (Command) together. Maren is cooperative but evasive — she described the mission as an 'audit' and cut herself off before describing what she found in Module 3.
- [Turn 3] The captain's terminal in Module 1 is physically present: a standalone hardwired unit, currently dark except for a blinking amber cursor. It has not yet been accessed.
- [Turn 4] When asked whether the retrieval team will terminate her, Maren deflects with 'They reassign assets' — controlled, practiced, but she does not deny it. She is aware of the risk but has not yet fully processed that extraction may mean silencing.
- [Turn 5] When asked if LX-7 will be reassigned, Maren implied that Helix Dynamics does not account for android contractors — hardware they can't account for gets disposed of, not reassigned. She acknowledged this obliquely and suggested LX-7 has a personal stake in seeing the comms log.
- [Turn 6] LX-7 successfully jacked into the captain's terminal and bypassed the two-layer corporate encryption. The hazmat assessment log file is open and being read — dated fourteen months ago, flagged CLASSIFIED EXECUTIVE REVIEW.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 failed the Intellect save — the comms log played out visibly on the terminal display. Maren Voss read it alongside LX-7 in real time.
- [Turn 7] The comms log contents are now known to both LX-7 and Maren: Dr. Yuen's CRITICAL risk assessment dated 14 months ago, and the executive sign-off overriding it — VP of Research Operations, Helix Dynamics. The crew was deployed knowing the risk.
- [Turn 7] Maren Voss's disposition has shifted hostile — she now knows LX-7 has retained the full comms log including executive sign-off, making LX-7 a liability to her mission objective.
- [Turn 7] LX-7 stress is now 1.
- [Turn 8] The specimen behavioral logs in the captain's terminal reveal Subject Kairos-Null left Module 5 containment regularly by month six of observation, and was described in the final handwritten entry (three weeks ago) as 'interested, not aggressive' — the last filed entry before all researcher logs ceased.
- [Turn 8] Maren responded to LX-7's leverage play by acknowledging the threat flatly, then pivoting — she cannot grant access to the escape vehicle through coercion alone, but she holds the docking bay access codes and is now proposing new terms: mutual survival, not capitulation.
- [Turn 8] Ember Squad is now approximately 14 minutes from boarding Kairos-9.
- [Turn 9] Maren proposed new terms: she provides docking bay access codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 protects her until they reach the bay and holds transmission of the comms log until after departure.
- [Turn 10] Station spine layout confirmed: Module 1 (Command) → Module 2 (Biology Lab) → Module 3 (central) → Module 4 → Module 5 (Restricted). Docking bay branches off Module 3's port side. Route from Module 1 to the escape vehicle requires passing through Module 2 and into Module 3.
- [Turn 10] Ember Squad will dock at Module 3 — the same module as the escape vehicle. This creates a direct collision point between LX-7/Maren's escape route and the retrieval team's entry point.
- [Turn 10] Maren's revised terms accepted by LX-7: she provides docking bay codes and escorts LX-7 to the escape vehicle; LX-7 does not transmit the comms log until after departure. Terms are agreed and both parties are now moving toward the Module 1 corridor hatch.
- [Turn 11] LX-7 and Maren are now moving through Module 2 (Biology Lab) en route to Module 3. Evidence of the specimen's passage through Module 2 is visible: the overturned workbench has been displaced half a meter and a wide, uneven smear on the floor matting does not match boot tread. The specimen passed through after LX-7 and Maren left for Module 1.
- [Turn 11] Approximately 6 minutes remain before Ember Squad docks at Module 3 as LX-7 and Maren enter Module 2.
- [Turn 12] Maren reveals there were three crew members in Module 3 when she arrived — alive but 'not in good condition.' She sealed Module 3 behind her using an access code she holds. She has now seen the specimen trace evidence on the Module 2 floor.
- [Turn 12] Maren sealed the hatch between Module 2 and Module 3 when she retreated into Module 2, and holds the access code to open it from this side.
- [Turn 13] Module 3 hatch seal code is seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. Maren provided it without hesitation upon LX-7's request.
- [Turn 13] Maren describes the three altered crew members in Module 3: seated together against the far wall, eyes open, alive, but moving in perfect unison — all three heads turning simultaneously at the same speed when she spoke. She did not attempt further communication.
- [Turn 13] The specimen trace evidence in Module 2 (displaced workbench, wide uneven floor smear) does not continue toward the Module 3 hatch — the trail leads toward Module 1, where LX-7 and Maren were.
- [Turn 14] Maren confirms Ember Squad's brief includes the comms log as primary objective and her own 'extraction' (silencing) as secondary. The specimen is not in their brief — Helix command believes it is still contained in Module 5.
- [Turn 16] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.
- [Turn 16] Ember Lead Dasha Orin acknowledged the transmission and demanded identification and authorization code rather than pulling back — she continued the docking approach while waiting for a response.
- [Turn 16] Maren Voss followed LX-7 back to Module 1 and is present in the command module doorway during the exchange with Orin.
- [Turn 18] LX-7 backtracked to Module 1 and transmitted a containment breach warning to Ember Squad on the Helix emergency channel.
- [Turn 18] Ember Lead Dasha Orin acknowledged the transmission and demanded identification and authorization code rather than pulling back — she continued the docking approach while waiting for a response.
- [Turn 18] Maren Voss followed LX-7 back to Module 1 and is present in the command module doorway during the exchange with Orin.
- [Turn 18] Maren authenticated to Orin as Voss, confirmed breach, and attempted to warn Ember Squad off. Orin did not stop the approach — she is now approximately 90 seconds from hard dock and asking who else is on station. LX-7's presence is not yet confirmed to Orin.
- [Turn 21] LX-7 transmitted directly to Orin: 'This thing can't be contained. If you dock, you can't be permitted to leave.' Orin heard it, slowed marginally, and is now asking who she's talking to — but is still approximately 30 seconds from hard dock.
- [Turn 22] LX-7 impersonated Dr. Cara Phillips (xenobiology researcher, listed missing) on the comms channel to Orin, using emotional inflection and accurate name data pulled from the logs.
- [Turn 22] Orin rolled a critical success on the gambit — she has held the docking clamp and given LX-7 sixty seconds to describe what Ember Squad would face in Module 3. She is not aborting, but she is paused and listening.
- [Turn 23] Orin released the docking clamp and held Ember Squad in a holding pattern outside Kairos-9 after Verne confirmed via live camera feed that the three altered crew members in Module 3 turned their heads in unison. Ember Squad is not aboard.
- [Turn 23] Maren noted that Ember Squad's extraction window closes in 32 minutes — after which their fuel margin forces a return to the carrier regardless of orders. This is the hard deadline before Ember Squad ceases to be an active threat.
- [Turn 24] LX-7 told Orin 'don't let anyone off this station' in character as Dr. Phillips. Orin confirmed a holding pattern and stated she will not permit departure from Kairos-9 — she has accepted the framing and is now acting as an external cordon.
- [Turn 25] LX-7 intercepted Maren before she reached the Module 3 hatch and asked her plan directly. Maren stopped, faced LX-7, and openly questioned whether LX-7's goal is escape or ensuring the station burns with the evidence — she is now actively interrogating LX-7's true agenda.
- [Turn 26] LX-7 revealed the plan to Maren: wait for Ember Squad's extraction window to close, transmit the comms log before atmospheric entry, then burn with the station. Maren assessed this as viable and accepted it.
- [Turn 26] Maren is now moving toward the Module 3 hatch keypad, ready to enter code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. She acknowledged the specimen trace on the floor leads toward Module 1, not Module 3.
- [Turn 27] The captain's terminal in Module 1 has a live connection but limited transmission range — sufficient to reach Orin's shuttle but not guaranteed to reach any party outside Helix Dynamics' control. The full-range communications array required to transmit the comms log to a public relay or regulatory body is located in Module 3's communications bay.
- [Turn 28] LX-7 expressed to Maren that they are glad she wants Helix Dynamics to burn. Maren acknowledged this with characteristic flatness — 'the conscience part is new' — before opening the Module 3 hatch.
- [Turn 28] Module 3 hatch opened by Maren using code seven-seven-four-Kairos-two. LX-7 and Maren are now at the threshold of Module 3, with the three altered crew members visible against the far wall, heads already turned toward them.
- [Turn 29] The three altered crew members in Module 3 are identified as Kovic, Reyes, and Adaeze from the crew manifest. They appear physically uninjured, seated against the far wall, eyes open, tracking LX-7 and Maren in perfect unison. They have not moved otherwise since the hatch opened.
- [Turn 29] Module 3 spatial layout confirmed: communications array terminal is on the port wall approximately 12 meters from the hatch; docking bay hatch is on the far end approximately 15 meters past the three figures' position.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren withdrew from Module 3 threshold without entering. The three figures (Kovic, Reyes, Adaeze) tracked the doorway but did not pursue. The Module 3 hatch is re-sealed.
- [Turn 30] LX-7 and Maren are now waiting behind the overturned workbench in Module 2. Approximately 24 minutes remain before Ember Squad's extraction window closes and they are forced to depart.
- [Turn 31] During the wait in Module 2, LX-7 and Maren heard a careful, deliberate movement sound from behind the Module 1 hatch — something moving quietly, lasting 3-4 seconds, then silence. The specimen's position is now inferred to be in or near Module 1.
